It started with the Ezrat Nashim, a massive courtyard where the nation gathered for epic celebrations like the Water Drawing Ceremony. 🎊 Then, you’d move up the steps to the Azarah, the inner courtyard where the Kohanim performed the sacred services and the massive Altar stood. 🔥 Inside the main Sanctuary building was the Kodesh, home to the golden Menorah that stayed lit 24/7, the Table with the twelve loaves of Showbread, and the Golden Altar for sweet-smelling incense! 🕯️🍞 But the absolute heart of the Temple was the Kodesh HaKodashim (Holy of Holies). This room was so sacred that only the High Priest could enter, and only once a year on Yom Kippur. 🕊️ Inside sat the Ark of the Covenant itself! 📜 Even though it’s not standing today, many Sages say the "Divine Presence" never left the area. Learn more about it! 🎊

The Torah commands Jewish men to wear fringes on the corners of four-cornered garments — a constant physical reminder of all G-d's commandments.

What tzitzit are:
Tzitzit (צִיצִית — fringes or tassels) are knotted strings attached to each corner of a four-cornered garment. Each set of tzitzit has 8 threads and 5 knots. A famous derash (Bamidbar Rabbah) links the gematria of tzitzit (600) plus 8 threads and 5 knots to 613 — a symbolic reminder of all the mitzvot, not a halachic calculation.

The Torah source:
"Speak to the Children of Israel and tell them to make fringes on the corners of their garments... so that you may look upon them and remember all the commandments of G-d." (Numbers 15:38-39)

How to observe it:
Most observant Jewish men wear a tallit katan (small garment) under their shirt all day. During morning prayers (Shacharit), a tallit gadol (large prayer shawl) is worn over the clothing.

Getting started:
• Buy a tallit katan at any Judaica store or online
• Ask your rabbi to show you how to wear it and say the blessing
• Check your tzitzit periodically — if a string breaks, it may invalidate them

revi'it — A revi'it (רביעית) is a standard unit of liquid volume in halacha, defined as one-quarter of a log — commonly translated as the volume of an egg and a half. It is the minimum volume required for ritual mitzvot involving liquids, such as Kiddush. Because physical volumes have shifted and been debated, several contemporary measurements exist depending on the posek followed: Rav Chaim Na'eh: ~86 ml (approx. 2.92 fl oz) — widely used as a lenient baseline, particularly in Sephardic tradition and for rabbinic obligations like netilat yadayim. Rabbi Moshe Feinstein: ~130–150 ml (approx. 4.4–5.1 fl oz) — many recommend this for Torah-level obligations like Shabbat Kiddush. The Chazon Ish: ~150 ml (approx. 5.1 fl oz) — stringent standard in many Ashkenazi and Hasidic communities for Torah-level mitzvot. Besides Kiddush, a revi'it is also required for Havdalah (minimum wine drunk from the cup), netilat yadayim (minimum water poured — may rely on the smaller measure), and the four cups at the Pesach Seder. After Kiddush, many require drinking melo lugmov or the majority of the cup; ask your rav which measure to use.

Learn about the incredible "sliding scale" system that made sure nobody was ever priced out of a fresh start! 🕊️ The Korban Oleh V'Yored is yet more proof that Hashem cares about each Jew! Depending on how much you had in your pocket, the price of your offering would literally move up or down! 📉📈 This flexible system applied to specific moments like accidentally entering the Temple or eating holy food while impure, forgetting a personal oath and accidentally breaking it, staying silent when you should have testified, healing from the spiritual skin condition Tzara'at, or even a new mother's offering after giving birth. If business was going well, you would bring an animal. But if things were a bit tight, you could offer just birds. In some cases, you could even just show up with a sack of flour! 🌾 Whether you were a high-roller or just getting by, the path back to holiness was always wide open. It wasn't about the cost of the gift, but the heart of the person bringing it! Your mission: think about how G-d is not like most human leaders: He truly wants to be connected with his subjects, instead of just with their money!

Learn about the mikveh — a bath with requirements so specific that rabbis are required to get the plumbing right! 💧 A mikveh is NOT just a pool. Jewish law has extremely precise requirements: (1) Minimum volume of 40 se'ah of water — about 200 gallons. (2) The water cannot be 'drawn' — water pumped directly from a tap into the mikveh is invalid! It needs to come from Heaven, meaning rainwater or possibly snow transported in a halachically appropriate manner. (3) The person must be completely immersed with NOTHING between their body and the water — which means no bandages, no nail polish, etc. Who uses a mikveh today? Women after their monthly niddah period (the primary use); converts completing conversion; some men also use it to attain a higher level of holiness. Many use it the day before Yom Kippur. Mikvehs are also used to immerse vessels bought from non-Jews (tevilat keilim — yes, you immerse your pots too!). Fun fact: when a new Jewish community is established, halacha says the mikveh gets built BEFORE the synagogue, because it's what allows husbands and wives to be together, which is a foundation of Jewish life.

Many poskim and communities hold that women should say Birchot HaShachar upon waking — the same sequence of morning blessings men recite at the start of Shacharit, thanking G-d for the gifts of each new day.

What they are:
Birchot HaShachar (בִּרְכוֹת הַשַּׁחַר — Blessings of the Dawn) is a sequence of ~15 short blessings. They originated as blessings said at each stage of waking — opening your eyes, standing up, getting dressed, and stepping outside.

Women's wording:
Use your siddur's women's version. Instead of "who has not made me a woman," women say "who has made me according to His will." The other blessings are the same.

When to say them:
• From chatzos (halachic midnight) onward.
• Ideally say HaNoten LaSechvi Vinah (the rooster blessing) after alot hashachar; if you said it after chatzos, you have still fulfilled your obligation.
• The other blessings may be said from chatzos if you wake up very early. Ideally say them upon waking.

Even if you do not daven full Shacharit, many say these blessings are an important way to start the day with gratitude.

Ask your rabbi or rebbetzin about your community's practice.

A kezayit is the olive-sized portion in Jewish law used to measure how much food you must consume for mitzvot — eating matzah at the Seder, maror, bentching after bread, and after-blessings. Because ancient volumes are difficult to calculate precisely, rabbis have established modern equivalents. Halachically, a kezayit is tied to the size of an egg — usually calculated as either half or one-third of an egg's volume depending on the authority. Many major organizations (such as Star-K) equate a kezayit to about 1.2 to 1.3 fluid ounces (approx. 35–40 ml), which visually translates to the size of a golf ball or a roll of quarters. By weight, this generally ranges between 25 and 33 grams — the stringent opinion of the Chazon Ish is approx. 33 g; the more lenient opinion of Rabbi Chayim Na'eh is approx. 27 g. (Note: For porous foods like matzah, the required weight is typically lower — around 15–20 grams depending on your rav.) The exact size depends on which authority you follow; ask your rav or use a community chart for the specific food or mitzvah you are measuring. Eating less than a kezayit may mean the mitzvah was not fulfilled.

The Torah commands the Shema to be said "when you lie down" — meaning in the evening. This is a separate Torah-level obligation from the morning Shema.

What it is:
The same three Torah paragraphs as the morning Shema (see the morning prayer section), but said at night with different surrounding blessings.

The blessings around the evening Shema:
• Before: A blessing about G-d alternating day and night (Ma'ariv Aravim) and a blessing about G-d's eternal love for Israel (Ahavat Olam)
• After: A blessing affirming the Exodus from Egypt (Emet Ve'Emunah) and prayers for protection through the night

Timing:
From nightfall (tzeit hakochavim — when three medium stars appear) until dawn. Ideally said before halachic midnight (chatzos halayla). The app shows the current window.

After sunset (shkiyah):
While Maariv (the evening prayer) can begin at sunset, the biblical obligation to recite Shema at night has not yet begun. If you pray early Maariv, you will need to repeat the Shema later (at nightfall / tzeit hakochavim).

Note:
The evening Shema (as part of Maariv) is distinct from the bedtime Shema (Kriat Shema al HaMitah). Both are required.

kezayit — A kezayit is the olive-sized portion in Jewish law used to measure how much food you must consume for mitzvot — eating matzah at the Seder, maror, bentching after bread, and after-blessings. Because ancient volumes are difficult to calculate precisely, rabbis have established modern equivalents. Halachically, a kezayit is tied to the size of an egg — usually calculated as either half or one-third of an egg's volume depending on the authority. Many major organizations (such as Star-K) equate a kezayit to about 1.2 to 1.3 fluid ounces (approx. 35–40 ml), which visually translates to the size of a golf ball or a roll of quarters. By weight, this generally ranges between 25 and 33 grams — the stringent opinion of the Chazon Ish is approx. 33 g; the more lenient opinion of Rabbi Chayim Na'eh is approx. 27 g. (Note: For porous foods like matzah, the required weight is typically lower — around 15–20 grams depending on your rav.) The exact size depends on which authority you follow; ask your rav or use a community chart for the specific food or mitzvah you are measuring. Eating less than a kezayit may mean the mitzvah was not fulfilled.

Learn about the Torah's obligations of respect for Torah scholars and the elderly — with specific, practical rules! 🙌 'You shall rise before the aged and honor the face of the elder' (Leviticus 19:32). Two separate mitzvot here: rising for the elderly and rising for Torah scholars — with different rules for each (customs do vary, ask your local rav). For ELDERS (over 70): you must stand, or at least rise up slightly as a token of respect when they approach within 4 cubits (about 2 meters or 6-8 feet). The standing obligation ends when they pass. For TORAH SCHOLARS (talmid chacham): you must stand (if you're physically able) even if they're younger than 70, as long as they have significant Torah knowledge. For a Gadol (one of the leading rabbis of the generation) you should stand up as soon as you see them enter the room, and not sit until they reach their place. It's also important not to make fun of or embarrass a Torah scholar. The Talmud says someone who does this does not have a portion in Olam Haba (the World to Come)! Your mission: next time you see an elderly Jewish person, practice showing them a little more respect. It's a mitzvah!

Learn about the Korban Tamid — the daily offering that doesn't skip a beat when the Temple is standing. This constant offering was brought to the Temple twice a day, every single day, without exception. Once after dawn and once in the afternoon. It was always a lamb within its first year of life, perfectly unblemished. The morning Tamid was the absolute first act of the day; no other offering was allowed before it. The afternoon Tamid was the grand finale, the very last act before sunset. The only exception was the Passover lamb, but otherwise, the Tamid of the afternoon closed the doors on all other sacrifices for that day. Even though the Temple isn't standing today, the rhythm lives on. Our Shacharit and Mincha prayers correspond directly to these two daily lambs. The Talmud in Berakhot debates whether we pray because the Patriarchs started the trend—Abraham in the morning, Isaac in the afternoon, and Jacob at night—or if we do it to replace the sacrifices. The answer is basically both! The Patriarchs gave prayer its soul, but the Tamid gave it its schedule. Now, instead of a lamb, we offer the words of our lips to keep the chain goin'. 🐑

Learn about the mezuzah — and the surprising halachic criteria that determine which doorways actually require one! 🚪 The Torah says 'write them on the doorposts of your house and your gates' (Deuteronomy 6:9). But not every doorway qualifies. The Rambam lists the conditions: the space must have a minimum area (4 by 4 cubits), a roof or ceiling, two proper doorposts, a lintel connecting them, and be used for regular living purposes. This means: a walk-in closet might require a mezuzah; a bathroom doesn't (due to dignity); a garage door might. PLACEMENT: on the RIGHT side as you enter, in the upper third of the doorpost, angled so the top points inward. Ashkenazim angle it at 45 degrees (compromise between the Rambam who says vertical and Rashi who says horizontal). Sephardim place it vertically. HEIGHT: must be in the top third of the height of the doorpost, or typically around an adult's shoulder height. The parchment inside must be written by a qualified sofer (scribe) — a printed mezuzah scroll is invalid. The mezuzah provides Divine protection for whoever has them on the required doorways in their house, even when they're not in the house!

Yom Ha'atzmaut (5 Iyar) commemorates Israeli independence in 1948. It is not a Biblical or Rabbinic Yom Tov — melacha (work) is fully permitted.

Customs vary significantly by community:

Religious Zionist / Modern Orthodox: Hallel is recited (instituted by the Chief Rabbinate of Israel). Whether Hallel is said with a bracha is disputed — the Chief Rabbinate instructed with a bracha; many Ashkenazi poskim outside Israel say without a bracha. Tachanun is omitted. Some communities add special festive prayers (Hallel u'Maariv).

Sephardic (Rav Ovadia Yosef / Yalkut Yosef): Rav Ovadia Yosef ruled that Hallel should not be recited (concern of bracha levatala since the day was not established by Chazal). Tachanun omission is also disputed in these communities.

Chabad: The Rebbe did not institute any special observance. Most Chabad communities do not say Hallel and recite Tachanun as usual.

Charedi communities (Agudah, Litvish): Generally do not observe the day as a religious holiday. Tachanun is said as usual.

The Omer continues to be counted normally. There is no Al HaNissim addition to davening.

Ask your rav which custom your community follows.

Married Jewish women are required to cover their hair whenever they are in public or in the presence of men other than their husband.

What it is:
Kisui rosh (כִּיסּוּי רֹאשׁ — literally "covering of the head") is one of the fundamental practices of Jewish married life. It is a Torah-level obligation (Shulchan Aruch, Even HaEzer 115:4).

Why:
A married woman's hair is considered ervah (עֶרְוָה — literally "nakedness"; in halachic terms, something private that should not be publicly displayed). Covering it expresses that her deepest beauty is reserved for the privacy of her home and her husband. It is also an expression of tzniut (modesty) — the Jewish value of inner dignity over external display.

Methods of covering:
• Tichel (טִיכֶל) — a headscarf or wrap, commonly used across many communities
• Sheitel (שֵׁייטֶל) — a wig; widely used in Ashkenazic and Chassidic communities
• Hat or beret
• Mitpachot — decorative wraps and turbans
• Combination (e.g., small wig with a hat over it)

Standards:
Most Orthodox authorities require the entire hair to be covered in public. Details vary by community. Speak with your rabbi or rebbetzin about what applies in your situation.

Learn about Kiddush Yadayim V'Raglayim — the mandatory sanctification of a Kohen's hands and feet before performing any sacred service in the Beit HaMikdash! 🌊 This crucial ritual was performed by placing the right hand over the right foot and the left hand over the left foot, allowing fresh water to pour over them simultaneously from a spigot on the copper Laver (Kiyor) or from any other sanctified vessel, poured either by himself or a colleague, as long as the flow was powered by direct human force (Koach Gava). Performing this act was an absolute requirement, and if a Kohen performed any service without first sanctifying his hands and feet, his service was completely invalid and disqualified. The sanctification would also be disqualified by any physical barrier like dirt or bandages on the skin (Chatzitzah), or instantly voided if he was up all night performing the service and then dawn broke the following morning. He would also need to wash again if the Kohen stepped outside the Temple courtyard, contracted ritual impurity, or even used the bathroom!. This elaborate process reminds us that divine service sometimes requires an uncompromised level of holiness. Learn more!

Learn about the ultimate "legal permit" to enjoy the world! 🍎 The Talmud says that everything on earth belongs to Hashem, so taking a bite without a blessing is like "stealing" from the King’s palace. Saying a bracha is essentially asking for permission and turning a physical snack into a spiritual moment! 🏛️ The system is incredibly precise: if it grows on a tree, you say "Ha'etz." If it comes from the ground, it's "Ha'adamah." For bread, you have the heavyweight "Ha'motzi," and for everything else—like meat, cheese, or chocolate—you say "Shehakol." 🕊️ But then there’s the VIP of the drink world: wine! Because wine is so special and used for holy moments like Kiddush, it gets its very own unique blessing: "Borei Peri HaGafen." 🍷 It’s so powerful that if you drink wine during a meal, it often covers all other drinks on the table! 🥂 There is even a specific "Order of Operations" called Mag'a Esh to help you decide which food gets the blessing first if you have a big plate. 🍇 It’s like a daily training session in mindfulness—stopping to recognize exactly where your food came from and all the amazing creations, tastes and flavors that Hashem put in the world for us to enjoy 🎊🥯.

Learn something interesting about kosher animals: As far as meat goes, in the whole entire world there aren't any animals allowed to be eaten by Jews aside from ten species listed in the Torah: Cows, Sheep, Goats, are the domestic ones. The wild ones are the gazelle, deer, antelope, ibex, chamois, bison, the giraffe (translations may be subject to dispute) and their subspecies. So why did Hashem create millions of species we aren't allowed to eat? Ponder this deep secret from the Torah! The Sifra, an ancient legal commentary, offers a powerful psychological perspective. A person should not say, 'I don't want to eat pork because it's disgusting.' Rather, one should say, 'I would like to eat it, but what can I do? My Father in Heaven has decreed against it.' Hashem created a world full of 'forbidden' things to give us the constant opportunity to choose His will over our own desires, transforming the simple act of not eating into a holy deed. Hashem wants to give us unfathomable rewards, in this world and in the World to Come. There are countless things in this world a person can do, let alone eat. If we do the few things that Hashem tells us are 'mitzvot' (His will), imagine the joy He gets!

Sukkot begins on 15 Tishrei and lasts seven days (in the Diaspora, the first two days are full Yom Tov, followed by five days of Chol HaMoed). It commemorates the 40 years the Jewish people dwelt in the desert under G-d's protection.

Key observances:
• Dwelling in a sukkah (a temporary outdoor booth with a roof of natural plant material) — at minimum, eating meals there
• Taking the Arba Minim (Four Species): lulav (palm branch), etrog (citron), hadassim (myrtle), aravot (willow)

Arba Minim — by nusach:
• Ashkenaz / most Sefard: waved in six directions (east, south, west, north, up, down) during Hallel; bracha on the first day (and in Chutz LaAretz, also on the second day of Yom Tov)
• Chabad: hold lulav in right hand, etrog in left; wave in the same six directions plus some add an extra motion; follow your Chabad siddur
• Yemenite (Baladi): may bind aravot differently and follow distinct waving customs — ask your community
• Not taken on Shabbat; in Israel, the first day is the primary Torah obligation; in Chutz LaAretz, the first two Yom Tov days

Hallel:
• Full Hallel every day of Sukkot in most communities
• Some Yemenite and other traditions differ on certain days — follow your siddur

Before eating or drinking anything, we say a short blessing (bracha) thanking G-d for the food. This is one of the most frequently-performed mitzvot in daily life.

What a bracha is:
Bracha (בְּרָכָה — blessing, plural: brachot) is a brief formula beginning "Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam..." (Blessed are You, L-rd our G-d, King of the universe) followed by a specific ending based on the food.

The Talmud's principle:
"It is forbidden to benefit from this world without a blessing." Eating without a bracha treats G-d's world as ownerless — the bracha is our acknowledgment that everything belongs to G-d.

The six main blessings before food:
• Hamotzi (הַמּוֹצִיא) — for bread made from any of the five grains (wheat, barley, spelt, oat, rye)
• Mezonot (מְזוֹנוֹת) — for other grain-based foods: cake, cookies, crackers, pasta, cereal
• HaGafen (הַגָּפֶן) — for wine or grape juice; the blessing is Borei pri ha'gafen (בּוֹרֵא פְּרִי הַגָּפֶן)
• HaEtz (הָעֵץ) — for fruit from a tree (apples, oranges, grapes, etc.)
• HaAdamah (הָאֲדָמָה) — for produce from the ground (vegetables, bananas, strawberries)
• Shehakol (שֶׁהַכֹּל) — for everything else: meat, fish, eggs, milk, cheese, water, candy, etc.

Tomorrow is $fastName — a public fast from dawn (alot hashachar) until nightfall (tzeit).

If you plan to eat before the fast begins:
• Set a mental condition (tanai) the night before: "If I wake up hungry before dawn, I will eat." Without this condition, waking early and eating may prohibit you from eating again until the fast officially begins at dawn (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 564:1).
• If you wake before dawn and want to eat, you may drink water and eat foods that are not normally cooked for a meal — e.g. a piece of cake, fruit, or cereal. A full hot meal is disputed; many avoid a formal cooked meal once they have decided to fast (Mishnah Berurah 564:8–9).
• Stop all eating and drinking at alot hashachar${alotTomorrow?.let { " (approx. $it — enable location for your exact zman)" } ?: " — enable location in Settings for your dawn time"}.

Practical prep tonight:
• Hydrate well and eat a balanced dinner.
• Plan a lighter morning if you will not eat before dawn.
• Know your synagogue schedule if you plan to attend special prayers.

Who must fast: Jewish adults (bar/bat mitzvah age and older) in good health. Children below bar/bat mitzvah are not required to fast — train them gradually per your rav.$fridayNote

Learn the step-by-step process of bringing a korban (offering) — and why reversing even one step could invalidate the whole thing! 🐑 Bringing a korban was a precisely choreographed process with four essential acts, in a specific order: (1) Shechitah — the slaughter, performed by any Israelite (not just a Kohen!). Any adult Jewish male could slaughter the animal. (2) Kabbalah — the Kohen catches the animal's blood in a sacred vessel immediately. This step MUST be done by a Kohen. (3) Holachah — the Kohen carries the blood-filled vessel to the altar. (4) Zerikah — the Kohen sprinkles the blood on the altar. If the steps were done out of order (e.g., a non-Kohen performed the kabbalah, or the blood was placed before the zerikah) — the entire korban was invalid ('pasul'). Another fascinating rule: intent matters throughout. If the Kohen performing the service THOUGHT about eating the meat after its permitted time window (known as 'pigul') — that mental disqualification could invalidate the offering even though he hadn't done anything wrong physically! The Torah treated the mind of the Kohen as part of the sacred act itself. Learn more about how sacrifices were offered in the Rambam's Hilchot Ma'aseh HaKorbanot!

Learn about Losh — kneading and forming a mass from separate ingredients! 🍞 Think dough: flour + water + mixing = one unified blob. In the Mishkan, Losh was a key agricultural step used when mixing water with the ground herb powders to make dyes, as well as kneading the flour to bake the Showbread. On Shabbat, this melacha prohibits combining a finely divided powder or small solid particles with a liquid to form a single, cohesive paste or thick mass. This restriction directly affects how people prepare everyday items like baby cereal, instant oatmeal, mustard, or even certain dense textures of egg salad. To avoid a Torah-level violation, the halacha requires using specific workarounds when a paste must be made, such as changing the normal order of ingredients (for instance, pouring the liquid into the bowl before adding the powder) and mixing the ingredients with an unusual motion (Shinui), like employing a crisscross slicing movement with a knife instead of a standard circular stir. This is why Shabbat challah is baked before the day of rest begins, since the creative act of unifying separate elements into a brand new entity belongs entirely to Friday, leaving us to enjoy the completed creation on Shabbat. Learn more!

Get ready for a hunt! Bedikat Chametz (Nighttime Search for Leaven)! 🔦 The night before Passover eve (the night of the 13th of Nissan), after dark, we conduct a formal search for Chametz (leavened food) by candlelight! Traditionally, a beeswax candle is used because it burns straight and illuminates well, but a flashlight works too. The search is a family affair, often involving a feather to sweep crumbs into a wooden spoon, which are then placed in a paper bag. A blessing is recited before the search. What's with the tradition of hiding ten pieces of bread? This clever custom ensures that the blessing isn't said in vain if you've already meticulously cleaned your home! After the search, a declaration (Kol Chamira) is made in Aramaic: 'Any chametz that is in my domain that I have not seen or removed — it is nullified and ownerless like the dust of the earth.' This 'Bitul' (nullification) is crucial, as it legally annuls your ownership of any inadvertently missed chametz. The next morning (Erev Pesach), any remaining chametz is burned (Biur Chametz), and a second declaration is made, covering chametz you might have eaten or missed. Learn more! Source: Mishnah, Pesachim 1:1; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 432-435; Talmud, Pesachim 4b.

Learn about the prohibition of blood — and the specific salting process that makes meat kosher to eat, including the one exception most people don't know about! 🧂 The Torah forbids eating blood ('the blood is the life'). So how do we remove it from meat? The melicha (salting) process: (1) Rinse the meat in cold water; (2) Soak in cold water for 30 minutes; (3) Cover all surfaces with COARSE salt (not table salt — it needs to draw out blood, not dissolve immediately); (4) Leave on a perforated board (so blood can drip off) for one hour; (5) Rinse thoroughly three times. Now the important exception most people don't know: LIVER cannot be koshered by salting. Liver is so full of blood that salt alone can't draw it out. Liver must be broiled directly over an open flame — the heat causes the blood to drip out. Only AFTER broiling can liver be cooked further in a pot. This is why 'kosher liver' from the butcher comes pre-broiled. One more time-sensitive detail: if meat was not koshered (salted) within 72 hours of slaughter, it generally CANNOT be koshered by salting anymore — only by broiling. So frozen meat must be koshered before freezing, or else can only be eaten broiled. Source: Leviticus 7:26-27; Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah 69-73.

Learn the Rambam's precise definition of teshuvah — and why verbal confession (vidui) is not optional! 🔄 The Rambam rules (Hilchot Teshuvah 1:1): teshuvah is a positive Torah commandment whenever one has sinned — whether a minor or major sin. The full process consists of four components: (1) Azivat HaChait — abandoning the sin. You actually STOP doing it, not just feel bad. (2) Charatah — genuine regret. Not just 'I got caught' regret, but authentic recognition that you did something wrong. (3) Kabalat l'atid — committing to change. Deciding that given the same circumstances, you would not repeat the sin. (4) Vidui — verbal confession out loud. Just feeling regret in your heart is NOT sufficient for teshuvah! The vidui must include: naming the specific sin, expressing regret, and committing to change according to the Rambam. He adds: repeating the same sin after teshuvah doesn't mean the earlier teshuvah was invalid — it just means you need to do teshuvah again. And again. As many times as necessary. The door never closes. For sins between people, there's a FIFTH requirement: you must first make restitution or seek forgiveness from the person you wronged. G-d does not forgive interpersonal sins without the person forgiving you first. Learn more!

The laws of family purity govern the intimate relationship between husband and wife and are among the most important mitzvot in the Torah.

What they are:
Taharat HaMishpacha (טָהֳרַת הַמִּשְׁפָּחָה — literally "family purity") is a cycle of physical separation and mikveh immersion that structures the intimacy of Jewish marriage.

The basic cycle:
• During niddah (נִדָּה — menstrual period and its aftermath), the husband and wife refrain from physical contact
• After the period ends, the wife counts seven "clean days" (shivah nekiyim) during which she checks that bleeding has fully stopped
• After the seven clean days, she immerses in a mikveh (ritual pool)
• After immersion, the couple resumes their relationship

Why these laws:
Jewish tradition teaches that these laws bring holiness, freshness, and blessing to marriage. Couples who practice them often describe the monthly rhythm of separation and reunion as one of the most surprisingly positive aspects of Jewish life.

How to learn:
This system is complex and must be learned properly before marriage — or as soon as you begin observance. Take a Taharat HaMishpacha course from a qualified teacher (rebbetzin, kallah teacher, or through organizations like Nishmat or local Jewish community programs).

Learn about the Nazirite vow — a unique spiritual journey! 🍇✂️💀 A Nazir (Nazirite) voluntarily undertakes three powerful abstentions for a minimum of 30 days: (1) No Grape Products: Absolutely nothing from the vine – no grapes, wine, grape juice, or even grape seeds/skins. This 'fence around the fence' prevents even a hint of indulgence. (2) No Haircuts: The Nazir allows their hair to grow wild, a visible sign of their dedication. At the vow's end, the hair is shaved and brought as an offering. (3) No Contact with the Dead: Even close family members (like a parent or child) cannot be touched, emphasizing a profound level of purity. The ultimate challenge: if the Nazir becomes ritually impure from a corpse, the clock resets! They must shave, bring sacrifices, and start the entire count over. The Torah calls a Nazir 'kadosh' (holy), sparking a fascinating debate among the Sages: Is this extra holiness praiseworthy? The Talmud embraces both perspectives. While no longer widely practiced (because there would be no way to offer the sacrifices required at the end of the nazirite vow period, since there's no Temple) the nazir teaches us about profound self-discipline and the pursuit of holiness. Source: Numbers 6; Rambam, Hilchot Nezirut 1; Talmud, Nazir.

Learn about the Tithe-to-Table system that turns every Israeli snack into a holy mission! 🧺 In the land of Israel, your fruits and veggies aren't just food—they are part of a massive spiritual cycle. Before you can take a single bite, you have to de-classify the produce from its restricted state called Tevel by separating several specific portions. 🏛️ The holy order of operations starts with Terumah Gedolah, a portion of the crop originally given to the Kohanim. Because it is super holy, only Kohanim in a state of purity could eat it (no one is in a state of purity today so we don't eat this nowadays). 🕊️ Then comes Maaser Rishon, where ten percent of what remains goes to the Levites. Next is the swing tithe called Maaser Sheni or Ani. In years one, two, four, and five of the Shemittah cycle, you would take another ten percent to Jerusalem for a holy feast! But in years three and six, that portion becomes Maaser Ani and goes straight to the poor. 🍎🤝 Even today, if you are shopping in an Israeli shuk, you might encounter Tevel, which is food that hasn't been processed yet. Modern halacha keeps the tradition alive by separating small symbolic amounts. It is like a spiritual tax that reminds us that the land doesn't belong to us, it belongs to Hashem!

Shavuot — receiving the Torah at Sinai. Yom Tov from tonight.

Tonight & tomorrow:
• Do not light candles, begin Maariv, or make Kiddush until full nightfall (tzeit) — see the timing note above. Shavuot is the only festival that may not begin before tzeit.
• Light Yom Tov candles at tzeit.
${shehecheyanuErevLines(HebrewCalendarEngine.SHAVUOS, tomorrowCal, profile)}
• Dairy is a cherished Shavuot minhag (cheesecake, blintzes). A festive meat meal with wine fulfills the primary mitzvah of Simchat Yom Tov (O.C. 529:2); many families have dairy first, then a full meat Yom Tov meal.
• All-night Torah learning (Tikkun Leil Shavuot) is a widespread custom tonight.
• Staying up all night: If you remain awake the entire night studying, standard Ashkenazi practice is to hear the morning blessings and Torah blessings from someone who slept, to avoid halachic doubts. However, according to Chabad custom (and several Sephardic authorities), you personally recite the entire sequence of morning and Torah blessings yourself after dawn (alot hashachar), even with zero sleep.
• Read Megillat Rut in many communities (tomorrow).
• Full Yom Tov davening with Full Hallel and Musaf; Akdamut/Megillat Rut per minhag.

No melacha; treat meals and prayer with joy and Torah focus.

Learn about tumat meit — ritual impurity from contact with death — and how it's actually the strongest form of impurity in all of Jewish law! ☠️ A corpse is called the 'father of fathers of impurity' (avi avot hatumah) — the absolute highest level of ritual impurity in the entire Torah system. Here's how it spreads: (1) Direct contact — touching a corpse makes you impure for 7 days. (2) Carrying — carrying a corpse even without touching also causes 7-day impurity. (3) Tent impurity (ohel) — this is the most famous rule: anyone or anything INSIDE the same enclosed space as a corpse becomes impure, even without touching it! So if someone dies in a house, everything in that house — people, utensils, furniture — becomes impure simultaneously. This is why Kohanim (priests) are forbidden from entering a house with a corpse, or even from walking over a grave. Kohanim keep this law today — which is why you'll see a Kohen take a long detour in a hospital (not walking through the building if someone may have died inside). The purification method? The Parah Adumah (Red Heifer ash water) — sprinkled on day 3 and day 7 of the 7-day impurity period. Without it, you stay impure indefinitely. Since we have no Red Heifer today, all Jews are considered to have tumat meit.

Learn the specific conditions under which speaking negatively about someone is PERMITTED — because lashon hara has important exceptions! 👄 The Chofetz Chaim (Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan, the definitive authority on lashon hara law) rules that negative speech is permitted — and sometimes obligatory — when ALL seven conditions are met: (1) The information must be TRUE — you verified it yourself. (2) It must be NECESSARY — there's a genuine toelet (constructive purpose), not just sharing information. (3) You must first try to solve the problem by speaking directly to the person who wronged someone, before reporting to a third party. (4) The information shared must be PROPORTIONATE to the actual harm — don't exaggerate. (5) Your intention must be purely for the benefit, not for personal satisfaction or revenge. (6) The damage caused by revealing it must not be GREATER than the damage prevented. (7) There must be no other way to accomplish the goal. The classic permitted case: warning someone that their potential business partner is a known fraudster. You MUST tell them — withholding the information would cause harm. So the prohibition of lashon hara is not 'never say anything negative' — it's 'never say anything negative without a real, valid purpose.' Learn more!

Shabbat is the holiest day of the week — the Jewish Sabbath. It begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars appear on Saturday night (approximately 25 hours).

What to prepare before Shabbat:
1. Cooking: All cooking must be done before Shabbat begins. A blech (a metal sheet covering the stove flame) or a pre-set hot plate keeps food warm on Shabbat without cooking.
2. Timers: Electric lights, the hot plate, and other appliances can be left on or set to turn on/off using a Shabbos timer (pre-set before Shabbat).
3. Lay out what you need: siddur (prayer book), Chumash (Torah book), clothes, children's items — anything you will want.
4. Candles: Light Shabbat candles 18 minutes before sunset (see next item).

On Shabbat — things we do NOT do:
• Write, draw, or erase
• Cook, bake, or light fire
• Turn electricity on or off (including phones, lights, and appliances)
• Drive or ride in a motorized vehicle
• Handle money or engage in commerce

On Shabbat — what we DO:
• Attend synagogue for Friday night and Saturday morning services
• Say Kiddush — a blessing over wine sanctifying Shabbat
• Eat three festive meals (Friday night, Saturday afternoon, and a third before nightfall)
• Study Torah, sing Shabbat songs (zemiroth), rest, and spend time with family

Learn about the divine command to have the best time ever! 🎊 The Torah doesn't just suggest you have fun on festival days—it literally commands it with the mitzvah of Simchat Yom Tov! This applies to the "Big Three" festivals: Pesach, Shavuot, and Sukkot! 🗓️ Since "there is no real joy without meat and wine," the holiday menu is basically a holy duty. 🍷🥩 But the halacha knows that everyone finds joy differently so it breaks down the party plan for the whole family! For men the focus is on delicious meat and wine. For women the mitzvah is fulfilled through getting new clothes or jewelry specifically for the holiday! For the kids it’s all about the sugar rush—giving them treats and nuts to keep the festive vibes high! 🍬 The man of the house is required to provide these things for his family according to his means! Our sages also warn that if you’re just feasting with your own family and locking the door you’re not doing "holiday joy" but just "stomach joy." To do it right you’ve got to pull up an extra chair for the poor and the lonely because true happiness is only real when it’s shared! At the very least you should give tzedaka to a Jewish person in need so they can enjoy the festival too. Learn more and get ready for the next festival, whenever that may be!

Kiddush Levana (Sanctification of the New Moon) — Birkat HaLevanah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt and the widespread custom is that women do not recite it at all (see Deracheha link below).

$waitLine

Deadline: The window ends at the moment of the full moon (roughly 14 days, 18 hours, and 22 minutes from the molad — about 14.75 days into the month). Saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late depending on the month. This app uses the Hebrew calendar day as a rough guide only — always check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location before the month ends.

When:
• After nightfall (tzeit), standing outdoors under the open sky.
• Ideally on Motzei Shabbat while still in nice clothes — a widespread custom because you are already dressed up.
• In Av, many wait until after Tisha B'Av; in Tishrei, many wait until after Yom Kippur — but say sooner on Motzei Shabbat if waiting risks cloudy nights.
• Not on Shabbat or Yom Tov itself.

How:
• The moon must be clearly visible — if clouds block it, wait for a clear night within the window.
• Use your siddur; it praises G-d for creation — not worship of the moon.

Window closes at the full moon (~14.75 days from the molad). Check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location.

Prepare for the ultimate harvest parade! 🍇 Learn about Bikkurim, the first-fruit offering that was one of the most emotional and high-energy ceremonies in the entire Temple service. When a farmer saw the very first ripening fruits of the Seven Species—wheat, barley, grapes, figs, pomegranates, olives, and dates—he would tie a reed around them to mark them as holy. 🎀 The journey to Jerusalem was a nationwide block party! 🥳 Pilgrims traveled together in massive groups, and as they entered the city, the local artisans and leaders would stop their work to stand up and welcome them! 🏛️ The streets filled with music from the flutes of the Levi'im as the parade headed toward the Temple Mount. The coolest part is the declaration the farmer made while holding his basket. He would recount the history of the Jewish people from the Exodus until that very moment of standing on his own land. 📜 Rich farmers brought their fruits in baskets of gold and silver, while the poor used simple wicker, but everyone’s offering was cherished. While we can't bring these physical fruits today without the Temple, this ceremony represents the ultimate goal of living with gratitude and national unity. It’s a beautiful preview of the celebrations we’ll see again when the third Temple is built! 🎊🍎.

Learn about the Torah obligation to TEACH — not just to learn — and the specific mitzvah of 'v'shinantam l'vanecha'! 📚 The Torah says 'And you shall teach them carefully to your children' (Deuteronomy 6:7). This is a positive commandment on every Jewish adult to teach Torah — and specifically, the obligation is first to your own children. The Rambam rules (Hilchot Talmud Torah 1:1): a father is obligated to teach his son Torah. If the father cannot teach, he must hire a teacher. When does the obligation begin? As soon as the child can speak! The first words you teach a child should be Torah — traditionally the Torah verse 'Torah tzivah lanu Moshe' and Shema. Extended meaning: the Talmud rules 'l'vanecha' means both biological children AND students. Teaching students is like raising spiritual children. The mitzvah also applies to each person themselves: you must learn in order to be able to teach. One fascinating ruling: you are NOT permitted to charge money for teaching Torah itself. A teacher may charge for their time away from other work (as opportunity cost), but direct payment FOR Torah teaching is frowned upon and considered a form of desecrating the Torah. This is why many Talmud teachers historically were supported by community funds rather than student tuition.

Candles are lit before sunset on Friday to usher in Shabbat and bring peace and warmth into the home. This is performed by the woman of the household (or a man if no woman is present).

There are two customs regarding the blessing:
• Ashkenazi custom: Light the candles first, then cover the eyes, recite the blessing, and uncover. This is done because reciting the blessing is considered accepting Shabbat — after which lighting would be forbidden. Covering the eyes allows the blessing to precede benefiting from the light.
• Sephardi custom: Recite the blessing first, then light the candles.

Every woman should follow the custom of her family. When in doubt, ask your rabbi.

Timing:
Candles are lit before Shabbat begins (before sunset). The amount of time before sunset varies by community:
• Most Ashkenazi communities outside Israel: 18 minutes before sunset
• Jerusalem: 40 minutes before sunset
• Many other communities: 20–22 minutes, or other local customs
Always check your local community Shabbat calendar for the exact time in your area.

Note: There is a strict earliest boundary for this mitzvah. You cannot light candles prior to Plag HaMincha (approximately 1.25 halachic hours before sunset). Lighting before this time invalidates the mitzvah and makes the blessing unnecessary.

$bedikatLeadIn

This year, Erev Pesach is on Friday (14 Nisan) and the first day of Pesach is Shabbat (15 Nisan):

• Bedikat chametz: Thursday night (night of 14 Nisan, after tzeit) — not Friday night. Recite the first bitul (Kol Chamira).
• Taanit Bechorot: Friday daytime (14 Nisan) — fast or attend a siyum.
• Biur chametz: Friday morning (14 Nisan) by the 5th halachic hour deadline. Both physical destruction and the final Kol Chamira nullification must be finished before this time.
• Mechirat chametz: must be entirely completed before Shabbat/Yom Tov candle lighting on Friday evening.
• First Seder: Friday night (commencing 15 Nisan).
• Second Seder (Diaspora only): Saturday night — transitioning directly from Shabbat into the second day of Yom Tov (not a regular weekend Motzei Shabbat). Kiddush includes Yaknehaz (integrated Havdalah: wine, Kiddush, candle, Havdalah text, Shehecheyanu — no spices/besamim).
• Preparation warning: you may NOT do any prep work (chopping, cooking, table setting) on Shabbat day for the second Seder. All preparations must wait until Shabbat ends at nightfall. Eruv tavshilin does not apply when Yom Tov falls on Shabbat — it is only for when Yom Tov immediately precedes Shabbat.

Confirm candle lighting, Yom Tov, and Seder times with your siddur and rav.

Learn about tochachah — the Torah obligation to rebuke someone doing wrong — and the limits that make it extremely difficult to fulfill in practice! 💬 The Torah commands: 'You shall surely rebuke your fellow' (Leviticus 19:17). The Rambam rules this is a positive commandment. But the halacha is full of conditions: (1) Tochachah must be given PRIVATELY — never in front of others. Shaming someone while rebuking them is such a serious violation that the Talmud compares it to murder. (2) You must rebuke in a CALM, gentle voice — not in anger or humiliation. (3) Here's the stunning rule: you must rebuke them again and again — 'even 100 times' if necessary — until either they accept it or they tell you to stop. (4) If the person will respond with insults and become MORE sinful, you are NOT obligated to rebuke. The point is to help, not to trigger. (5) If the person is committing the sin out of ignorance (they don't know it's wrong), there's an opinion that you should leave them be — 'it is better they sin in ignorance than sin defiantly.' The Talmud says: Rabbi Tarfon declared that 'I wonder if there is anyone in this generation who knows how to give tochachah' — implying that doing it correctly (without shaming, at the right time, in the right way) is extraordinarily difficult. Learn more!

Giving charity (tzedakah — צְדָקָה) is a constant mitzvah. A foundational custom is maaser kesafim (מַעֲשֵׂר כְּסָפִים) — setting aside about ten percent of one's income for charity.

How to calculate:
Maaser is generally figured from net earnings, not gross: start with what you actually receive, then deduct taxes, necessary work-related expenses, and reasonable business overhead before taking ten percent. Ask your rabbi for guidance on your specific situation (self-employment, benefits, one-time windfalls, etc.).

Where to give:
Halachic priorities place local poor individuals, Torah institutions, and the needy of Israel at the top of the list. Excellent choices often include local family relief organizations, community food pantries, and trusted charities that serve those in need in your area and in Israel.

If money is tight:
Jewish law teaches that your own livelihood and basic needs come first. If you cannot comfortably afford the full ten percent without real hardship, you are not expected to compromise your financial stability. Give a minimal, symbolic amount when you can so you still take part in this vital mitzvah — and increase when circumstances allow.

This checklist item is a daily reminder to plan your giving with intention. Confirm amounts and recipients with your rabbi.

Dive into the deep end and learn the kosher fish signs that determine if your seafood platter is a go! 🐟 For a fish to be kosher, it needs two specific features: fins and scales. While the fins help it navigate, the Talmud teaches that if a fish has valid scales, it is guaranteed to have fins too! These aren't just any scales, though; they must be visible to the naked eye and easily removable without tearing the skin. 🛡️ Symbolically, the Sages teach that scales represent a coat of armor for spiritual protection against outside influences, while fins represent the ambition and direction needed to swim against the current of life. Interestingly, some fish are masters of disguise! The swordfish is a major point of debate because it has scales as a juvenile but loses them as it grows up. ⚔️ Then you have the sturgeon, whose bony ganoid scales are so stuck on that most rabbis rule it non-kosher, which is why your high-end kosher caviar is usually from a different fish like paddlefish or salmon! 🥯 Did you know? Even though most blood of animals and birds is forbidden to eat (that's why we salt our meat, to remove the blood), fish blood is actually permitted to eat (you're just supposed to leave some fish scales in there so people don't think you're drinking forbidden blood)! Bottoms up! Learn more!

Learn about 'avak lashon hara' — the 'dust of negative speech' — and the specific patterns the Chofetz Chaim identifies as violations! 🌫️ Even when speech isn't explicitly negative, the Chofetz Chaim forbids several 'borderline' patterns: (1) 'He turned out okay' — damning with faint praise. 'Well, I'm surprised he actually delivered the project on time' clearly implies you expected him to fail. (2) 'Oh, him? Don't ask me about him!' — pretending to hold back while making the listener think there's something terrible to say. (3) Mentioning context that makes someone look bad indirectly: 'You went to Shimon's house? You know where he lives — the neighborhood with all the... well.' (4) Praising someone in front of their enemies — knowing that the enemy will respond by saying bad things about them. You triggered the lashon hara even though you technically said something positive. (5) Talking about someone's embarrassing past after they've repented and changed — bringing up their old behavior is avak lashon hara even if technically true. The underlying principle: even speech that isn't explicitly negative can still damage someone's reputation, destroy relationships, or trigger further gossip. The Torah's standard is not just avoiding lies — it's avoiding damage through words, even indirect damage.

${arbaMinimSharedBody(profile)}

Men — Torah obligation:
• The first day of Sukkot worldwide (15 Tishrei) is the Torah-level day for men. In the Diaspora, the mitzvah continues rabbinically on the second day of Yom Tov and Chol HaMoed.
• Bracha: Men say Al netilat lulav every day when taking the lulav (except Shabbat). Shehecheyanu is on the first day only.
• ${arbaMinimMenWave(profile)}

The ownership rule (lakhem — u'lekachtem lakhem):
• In Israel: the strict requirement to fully own your lulav and etrog set applies on the first day of Sukkot (15 Tishrei; Shulchan Arukh O.C. 658:3). If you do not own a set, ask the owner for matana al menat lehachzir (gift on condition of return) before you wave.
• In the Diaspora: because the second day of Yom Tov is kept as a safek de'oraita, the same lakhem ownership requirement applies on both Day 1 and Day 2 — you cannot simply borrow a synagogue set; it must be given to you as matana al menat lehachzir.
• In the Diaspora from the third day onward (the first day of Chol HaMoed): you may borrow a shared or synagogue set without a formal gift — the rabbinic continuation no longer carries the Torah ownership requirement (Peninei Halakha, Laws of Sukkot 13:13).
• In Israel on Chol HaMoed: borrowing without a gift is permitted — ownership was required only on the first day.

Learn about Borer — the famous 'selecting' melacha that changes how you eat on Shabbat! 🥗 Borer forbids separating a mixture of different types of objects, specifically by picking what you don't want out of what you do want. For an act of selection to be completely permissible on Shabbat, it must fulfill three strict conditions simultaneously: you must select the 'good' from the 'bad' (Ochel Mitoch Psolet), you must do it by hand (B'yad), and it must be for immediate use (M'yad). The first condition means you can only pick out the items you actually want to use or eat right now, leaving the unwanted items behind in the mixture; pulling the unwanted items out to leave the desired ones clean is a direct violation. The second condition, B'yad, means the selection must be done with your hands or a standard eating utensil like a fork, rather than using a dedicated sorting tool like a strainer, sieve, or peeler, which automatically transforms the act into a forbidden creative labor. The third condition, M'yad, dictates that the selection must be done for immediate or upcoming use—specifically for the meal happening right now—meaning you cannot sort a bowl of food in the morning to serve for lunch hours later. The law of Borer doesn't only apply to food, but anything that's in a mixture, like clothes, toys, etc. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Menapetz—the art of combing and separating raw fibers—and how a textile technique from the ancient desert shapes what we can and cannot do on Shabbat! This creative labor traces back to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers had to aggressively brush and comb raw wool or flax to untangle the knots, separate the individual strands, and align them so they would be perfectly straight and ready for spinning into beautiful fabrics. On Shabbat, Menapetz prohibits any action that mimics this industrial refinement of raw, tangled materials into a workable form. While the primary focus of this Melacha is manufacturing textiles, it also creates fascinating guidelines for personal grooming. Because pulling a hard-bristled brush through tangled hair will almost inevitably tear out hairs—which touches on a different prohibition called Gozez, or shearing—the spirit of Menapetz reminds us that we treat ourselves gently on this holy day. Instead of heavy brushing that forcefully separates strands, people use soft brushes or wide-toothed combs gently on dry hair to avoid industrial-style detangling. It turns a simple morning routine into a mindful reminder of the ancient desert workshop, showing how even the way we manage a bad hair day can reflect the deep rest and tranquility of Shabbat Learn more!

Mikra Megillah (hearing the Book of Esther) is a rabbinic mitzvah instituted for Purim (Esther 9:28; Megillah 19a). Men and women are equally obligated.

When to hear it:
• Once on Purim evening — after nightfall (tzeit)
• Once on Purim day — you are halachically obligated to hear the Megillah during the daytime (usually right after morning Shacharit and before sunset). The daytime reading is the primary fulfillment of the mitzvah (mitzvat ha'yom); the night reading was instituted later — both are required.

How to fulfill:
• Hear every word read from a kosher megillah scroll by someone who can discharge your obligation (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 690)
• Stand for the blessings; customs when Haman's name is read vary by shul (many make noise; sitting vs standing — follow your community)
• Listen without talking — missed words may require hearing that passage again; ask your rabbi if unsure

Blessings before reading:
• Al mikra megillah
• She'asa nissim
• Shehecheyanu (recited on the first night; Ashkenazim recite it by day as well)

Machatzit haShekel: A widespread pre-Purim custom (not one of the four Purim mitzvot in the same way); many give before Megillah — follow your community. Confirm local reading times with your shul.

Prayers & meals:
• Insert Al HaNissim into every Amidah and into Birkat Hamazon (bentching) all day long on Purim.

Yom HaZikaron (4 Iyar) is Israel's national day of remembrance for soldiers of the Israel Defense Forces and victims of terrorism who gave their lives for the State of Israel. It was established by the Knesset in 1963 and always falls the day before Yom Ha'atzmaut.

This is not a Yom Tov — melacha is fully permitted. It is a national civil observance.

Date adjustment: KosherJava adjusts the date when 4 Iyar falls near Shabbat, to keep Yom HaZikaron and Yom Ha'atzmaut together on consecutive days and not adjacent to Shabbat.

In Israel: Memorial sirens sound at 8:00 PM (start of the day, at nightfall) and again at 11:00 AM the following morning. Ceremonies are held at military cemeteries across the country. Flags fly at half-mast.

Prayers (Religious Zionist / Dati Leumi): Tachanun is recited fully at Shacharit — the day is one of solemn national memory. Tachanun is omitted only at Mincha as the calendar transitions into Yom Ha'atzmaut celebrations (Peninei Halakha 5:4:11; Koren Yom Ha'atzmaut mahzor — Mincha ketana before nightfall). Communities that do not treat Yom Ha'atzmaut as a religious day say Tachanun at Mincha too.

Most Charedi and Chabad communities: Do not observe as a religious day; regular weekday davening with Tachanun throughout the day.

The day ends at nightfall with the transition into Yom Ha'atzmaut celebrations.

$opener

Havdalah inside Kiddush (Yaknehaz):
• When a biblical holiday begins Saturday night (including Rosh Hashana), that night's Kiddush incorporates havdalah for Shabbat. The order is: (1) wine blessing, (2) holiday Kiddush, (3) candle blessing, (4) havdalah blessing — with the Yom Tov text concluding bein kodesh l'kodesh (not bein kodesh l'chol), (5) Shehecheyanu for the festival.
• This is the YaKNeHaZ order (Yayin, Kiddush, Ner, Havdalah, Zeman) codified in Shulchan Arukh for Motzei Shabbat into Yom Tov.
• Spices are not used.
• Use the Machzor or siddur nusach for Rosh Hashana — do not rely on memory for the long havdalah text.

Before or at Maariv:
• You may say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh to begin Yom Tov-permitted activities before Kiddush, or rely on Vatodi'enu in the Amidah of Maariv — follow your community.

$prepLead:
• Have round challah, honey, apples, and symbolic foods ready for the Yom Tov meals after Shabbat (minhag).
• Confirm shofar and Musaf times for the first day(s) of Rosh Hashana after Shabbat — shofar is not blown on Shabbat itself.
• Tashlich: when the first day of Rosh Hashana is Shabbat, it is postponed to Sunday (structural rule — avoiding carrying machzorim in public without an eruv).

Candles: after Shabbat ends, light Yom Tov candles from a flame lit before Shabbat began (pre-existing flame).

Learn about Birkat Kohanim — the priestly blessing — and the dramatic duchan ceremony that makes it so powerful! 🙌 The Torah gives the Kohanim a specific three-verse blessing to bestow on Israel (Numbers 6:24-26). In the Ashkenazic tradition, Birkat Kohanim is generally performed only on Yom Tov in regular synagogues outside Israel. In Israel, and among Sephardim everywhere, it is performed every single day at Shacharit. The ceremony: the Kohanim (descendants of Aaron the High Priest) remove their shoes, wash their hands (washed by Levites in many communities — an echo of the Temple service!), and ascend the 'duchan' (raised platform). They cover themselves with their tallits, spread their fingers in the special pattern, and sing the three verses while the congregation faces them with eyes closed. The Talmud (Sotah 39a) says: while the Kohanim bless Israel, the congregation should not look at the Kohanim's hands, because the Divine Presence rests upon them. The fingers form specific shapes — the pattern many may recognize as the 'Live Long and Prosper' Vulcan salute from Star Trek (fun fact: Leonard Nimoy adapted it from his childhood memories of watching Kohanim duchan!). Another interesting halacha: a Kohen who has committed murder cannot perform Birkat Kohanim — 'hands soaked in blood cannot bless' (until he does teshuva and repents).

Learn about the prohibition of receiving lashon hara — which is equally serious to speaking it, and which is actually harder to avoid! 🤐 The Chofetz Chaim rules: it is forbidden to LISTEN to lashon hara and believe it. More specifically, it's forbidden to: (1) Listen attentively and encourage the speaker to continue; (2) Enjoy hearing the negative information; (3) Believe what you've heard as established fact. The Talmud says a person who listens to lashon hara is actually worse than the speaker, because the speaker can repent by telling the person he spoke to that he made it up — but the listener has already received the information into his mind and the damage to his perception is done. Practical implications: If someone starts telling you something negative about a third party and you're stuck in the conversation — you have two levels of obligation: (a) Try to redirect or exit. (b) If stuck, consciously decide NOT to believe what you're hearing. You can listen without believing. The Chofetz Chaim calls this 'hearing without accepting.' One key insight: we are commanded NOT to believe lashon hara about another Jew — even if we can't immediately disprove it. We must maintain the assumption of innocence. This is extremely counterintuitive to modern ears, but it's actual halacha. Source: Chofetz Chaim, Hilchot Rechilut; Talmud, Niddah 61a.

Simchat Torah (23 Tishrei in the Diaspora) — rejoicing with the Torah.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Kiddush, festive meals, joy.
• Sukkah: Most Ashkenazim do not eat in the sukkah on Simchat Torah (it is no longer Sukkot) — Sefardic practice varies; follow your rav.
• Davening: Full Hallel and Musaf (like other Yom Tov days).

The mitzvah of the day — simcha and Torah:
• Hakafot — circling the bimah with Torah scrolls, singing and dancing (often many circuits).
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Complete the last parsha of Devarim and begin Bereshit — the Torah never ends.
• Aliyot — Kol HaNearim (all children together; an adult or bar mitzvah boy recites the blessing with them), Chatan Torah, Chatan Bereshit, and honors for members.
• Many shuls extend Hakafot to the evening or multiple sessions.

Practical tips:
• Dress festively; arrive early for a good spot.
• If you have a Torah honor, prepare your brachot.
• Celebrate responsibly — the day is about love of Torah, not excess.

Candle lighting last night of Yom Tov; havdalah when Yom Tov ends (often with Sukkot/Yom Tov additions in havdalah — use your siddur).

Erev Tisha B'Av prepares us for mourning the destruction of the Temple.

When the fast begins:
• Tisha B'Av starts at sunset tonight${sunset?.let { " (approx. $it)" } ?: ""} and continues until nightfall tomorrow night — not dawn to dusk like minor fasts.

Restrictions from chatzos (halachic midday) today:
• From chatzos onward: do not eat meat or drink wine (some extend other restrictions — follow your minhag).
• Reduce pleasurable activities; avoid laundry, haircuts, and swimming per mourning customs.

Seudah hamafseket — the final meal before the fast:
• Eat a simple meal alone, sitting on the floor or a low stool, with only one cooked dish (e.g. a hard-boiled egg dipped in ashes, or bread with cold water).
• Do not eat two cooked dishes together; do not recline; do not greet others with "hello."
• Finish before sunset; afterward, only water is permitted until the fast begins.
• Many recite Birkat HaMazon and then change into non-leather shoes before sunset.

After sunset tonight until nightfall tomorrow:
• Five afflictions apply (like Yom Kippur): no eating/drinking, no leather shoes, no bathing for pleasure, no anointing, no marital relations.
• Additionally: no Torah study except sad topics (Eichah, Job, mourning laws); many sit on the floor until chatzos tomorrow; kinot are recited.$shabbatNote

Ask your rav for details if you are ill, pregnant, or nursing.

Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — Fast of the Firstborn — is observed on Erev Pesach day by firstborn males (and some communities include firstborn females — ask your rav).

Why:
• Commemorates the plague of the firstborn in Egypt, when Jewish firstborn were spared.

The fast:
• When Erev Pesach is Shabbat (14 Nisan), Taanit Bechorot is moved to Thursday (12 Nisan) per Rama and Peninei Halakha — not Friday or Shabbat. Attend a siyum that day if that is your minhag.
• Many firstborns avoid fasting entirely by attending a siyum (completion of a Talmud tractate or similar Torah work) followed by a seudat mitzvah (festive meal). The exemption: To be exempt from the fast, you must not only hear the conclusion of the siyum but also participate in the seudat mitzvah by eating at least a kezayit (approx. 1 oz) of bread or cake. Leaving after the siyum without eating does not exempt you — you must still fast.
• If you fast: the fast runs from dawn (alot hashachar) until full nightfall (tzeit) — no eating or drinking in between.

Father of a firstborn son under bar mitzvah:
• If you are a man with a firstborn son below bar mitzvah age, the widespread custom is that you fast or attend a siyum yourself on his behalf — the young child does not observe the fast.

Plan ahead: locate a community siyum in advance if that is your minhag, or confirm fasting rules with your rav.

Learn about the Melacha of Boneh—the deliberate act of building or assembling a structure—and discover how this manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we handle household items, furniture, and temporary shelters on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans erected the massive wooden wall panels and fit the heavy foundation sockets together to create a unified, sturdy Tabernacle. The core definition of Boneh is the creation, improvement, or assembly of any structure, vessel, or functional space by combining parts together into a stable unit. On Shabbat, this means that heavy construction, installing permanent shelving, or repairing a loose door handle that fell out of its socket is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be pouring concrete on Saturday, Boneh introduces highly practical guidelines for routine household adjustments. A classic example is setting up a pop-up canopy or a large, multi-panel tent for shade; because you are assembling a covered shelter that shields a significant area from the sun or rain, it can constitute the creation of an Ohel (tent/canopy), which falls under the restrictions of building. Similarly, snapping together modular plastic furniture or attaching/screwing parts that form a rigid, tight unit are all problems. Learn more!

$omerTrigger

Set up before Yom Tov:
• Matzah — shmurah matzah for motzi/matza mitzvah (three matzot on the plate per custom)
• Maror — romaine, horseradish, or bitter herbs per your minhag
• Four cups of wine per participant (grape juice is widely used if needed — ask your rav; not the same debate as Chol HaMoed wine)
• Haggadah for each person (or shared)
• Seder plate: zeroa (shankbone), beitzah (egg), karpas, charoset, maror, chazeret
• Seder plate prep: You should ideally roast your zeroa (shankbone) on Erev Pesach day before sunset. Because the shankbone is not eaten on Seder night, roasting it after the holiday begins violates Yom Tov cooking laws. The egg (beitzah), however, is traditionally eaten during the meal, so it may legally be boiled or roasted on Yom Tov night if needed.
• Reclining (hasebha): Recline to the left when drinking the four cups and eating matzah, korech, and afikoman — do not recline while eating maror or chazeret (they symbolize slavery).
• Festive table; candles for Yom Tov

Kitchen:
• Only kosher-for-Passover food and utensils from this point
• Warm food on a blech or pre-set timer for Yom Tov meals

At the Seder: follow your Haggadah step by step — Kiddush, the order of the night, brachot, and the four cups are all laid out there. ${if (profile.isInIsrael) "One" else "Two"} Seder night(s) this Pesach.

Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when the new moon is visible at night. It is not worship of the moon — it praises G-d for creation's cycles and Israel's renewal. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 42a) compares one who blesses the moon properly to greeting the Shechinah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt (like most time-bound mitzvot) and the widespread longstanding custom is that women do not recite Kiddush Levana at all — prominent authorities (Shelah, Magen Avraham O.C. 426:1) advise against it for kabbalistic reasons as well. Say it outdoors under the open sky when possible, standing, after nightfall. Ashkenazim and Chabad often wait at least three days after the new moon; most Sephardim wait seven (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 426:4); Moroccan and some North African kehillot may begin after three days (Peninei Halakha 05-01-18). Motzei Shabbat is a widespread preference when people are dressed up and the moon is visible — but if waiting risks a string of cloudy nights and missing the monthly window, say it on the first clear weeknight the mitzvah becomes possible. The window ends at the moment of the full moon (roughly 14.75 days into the month) — saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late; check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location. Also called Birkat HaLevanah.

Learn about the Melacha of Makeh B'patish—the final hammer blow that completes a craft—and discover how this creative boundary pauses the finishing touches on all our physical objects on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled craftsmen would strike a final, decisive blow with a hammer on newly forged metal vessels to smooth out any rough imperfections and render the item fully functional. The core definition of Makeh B'patish is the intentional execution of any final action that takes an incomplete, flawed, or unusable object and transforms it into a finished, ready-to-use product. Under Torah law, this applies to any craft—whether it is snipping a loose, dangling thread off a freshly tailored shirt, inserting a screw to stabilize a table, bending a bent spoon back into position, or forcefully snapping a detached zipper pull back onto its track to make it work again. Similarly, fixing an item by removing a defect—such as removing a hazardous, protruding nail from a wall to make the surface safe and smooth—is viewed as a finishing act of repair. To navigate these boundaries smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals leave all minor repairs, assembly projects, and finishing touches until after Havdalah, fully enjoying our physical environment exactly as it was prepared before the holy day! Learn more.

Kiddush Levana — Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when the new moon is visible at night. It is not worship of the moon — it praises G-d for creation's cycles and Israel's renewal. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 42a) compares one who blesses the moon properly to greeting the Shechinah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt (like most time-bound mitzvot) and the widespread longstanding custom is that women do not recite Kiddush Levana at all — prominent authorities (Shelah, Magen Avraham O.C. 426:1) advise against it for kabbalistic reasons as well. Say it outdoors under the open sky when possible, standing, after nightfall. Ashkenazim and Chabad often wait at least three days after the new moon; most Sephardim wait seven (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 426:4); Moroccan and some North African kehillot may begin after three days (Peninei Halakha 05-01-18). Motzei Shabbat is a widespread preference when people are dressed up and the moon is visible — but if waiting risks a string of cloudy nights and missing the monthly window, say it on the first clear weeknight the mitzvah becomes possible. The window ends at the moment of the full moon (roughly 14.75 days into the month) — saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late; check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location. Also called Birkat HaLevanah.

Make an appointment for a private audience with the King of Kings! 👑 The Shemoneh Esrei—also known as the Amidah—is the heart of every Jewish prayer service. Our Sages composed it with "Ruach HaKodesh" (Divine Inspiration), meaning every word is a precise key designed to unlock heavenly gates and bring down spiritual and material blessings! 🔓 Because it is a direct conversation with Hashem, the Halacha has some "royal protocol" rules to keep it focused. First, you have to stand with your feet together, mimicking the angels who have "one leg," to show total devotion (and that you ain't goin' nowhere while talking to the King!). 😇 You also face Jerusalem—no matter where you are in the world—to point your heart toward the site of the Holy Temple! 🏛️ The prayer is split into three sections: three blessings of praise, thirteen requests for things like wisdom, health, and prosperity, and three final blessings of gratitude. On Shabbat and holidays, the format of the middle blessings are changed, so you make sure you're reading the correct Amidah from your siddur! 📜 While concentration (Kavanah) is great to have throughout, the Sages say it is most critical in the very first blessing of "Avot"—if you lose focus there, you’ve missed the core of the encounter (Ashkenazim don't go back to repeat, but Sephardim do)! 🕊️ Enter a different dimension and bring down all the blessings Hashem wants to give you! Learn more! 🎊🙏.

Learn about the gid ha-nasheh — the forbidden sciatic nerve — and the remarkable fact that this is one of the only mitzvot the Torah explicitly gives a reason for! 🦵 The Torah says: 'Therefore, the children of Israel do not eat the gid ha-nasheh which is on the hollow of the thigh, to this day, because he (the angel) struck the hollow of Yaakov's thigh on the gid ha-nasheh' (Genesis 32:33). Yaakov wrestled with a divine being all night and was struck on the hip, leaving him limping. In commemoration of this struggle — and Yaakov's perseverance despite the injury — the Torah forbids eating this specific nerve. Two things make this unusual: (1) The mitzvah was actually commanded before Sinai — it appears in Genesis, not Leviticus! The Talmud discusses whether the formal prohibition began at Sinai or at the moment of Yaakov's struggle. (2) The Torah TELLS US WHY — most mitzvot don't come with explicit reasons. Practical kashrut implications: the gid ha-nasheh runs through the entire hindquarter of an animal. Removing it properly requires a highly trained specialist called a 'menaker' (deveiner). The process is so complex that many kosher operations in the Diaspora (especially Ashkenazic communities) simply don't bother — they sell the entire hindquarter to non-Jews rather than attempt to remove the nerve. In Israel, menakers are more commonly available, so hindquarter beef is more widely available in kosher markets there.

Shemini Atzeret / Simchat Torah (22 Tishrei in Israel) — one day combining both.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; festive meals with Kiddush and Shehecheyanu (if not said already).
• Sukkot has ended — in Israel, do not eat or sleep in the sukkah today; festive meals are indoors (not in the sukkah).

Davening highlights:
• Liturgical shift: During Musaf today, the entire Jewish world officially transitions to the winter prayer cycle, universally inserting "Mashiv HaRuach U'Morid HaGeshem" into the second blessing of the Amidah. Tefillat Geshem (the formal prayer for rain) is recited in Musaf.
• Yizkor — memorial prayer in many Ashkenaz communities.
• Hakafot — dancing with Torah scrolls; finish the annual Torah reading and begin Bereshit again.
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Full Hallel and Musaf; Yom Tov Amidah.

Simchat Torah joy:
• Everyone receives an aliyah in many shuls; Kol HaNearim (children's aliyah) with flags — an adult (or a boy over bar mitzvah) stands with the group and recites the Torah blessing aloud so the aliya is halachically valid.
• Singing, dancing — honor the day with Torah celebration.

Evening: Candle lighting and Yom Tov; morning services are long — plan accordingly.

Learn about the Rambam's specific, practical description of what the Messianic era will actually look like — you might be surprised how down-to-earth it is! 🌍 The Rambam's famous ruling (Hilchot Melachim 11-12): 'The Messianic King will arise and restore the monarchy of David to its former glory... He will rebuild the Temple and gather all of Israel.' But then the Rambam adds something remarkable: 'Do not think the world will depart from its normal course in the Messianic era... A wolf will not dwell with the lamb — this is a metaphor for Israel dwelling peacefully among the nations.' The Rambam explicitly rules that the Messianic era will be a NATURAL time, not supernatural. Trees will still need to be planted, the sun will rise and set normally. The difference: Israel will be free from foreign oppression, and will be able to devote themselves to Torah study without distraction. 'In that time there will be no hunger, no war, no jealousy, no conflict — for good things will be plentiful, and all delicacies available like dust.' And the ultimate purpose: 'The sages and prophets did not yearn for the Messianic era in order to rule the world or to eat, drink, and be merry — but in order to be free to study Torah and wisdom, with no one oppressing them.' There are other rabbis who disagree and hold that the world will not be so normal, and instead that it will be a time of open miracles. Which one do you think would be better?

Learn about the ultimate "handle with care" policy for Hashem's names! ✍️ The Torah commands "you shall not do so to Hashem your G-d," which the sages explain as a strict prohibition against erasing or destroying His holy Name. This isn't just about being careful—it’s about treating the physical representation of the Divine with the highest level of honor! 🏛️ The halacha identifies seven specific Names that are so holy they can never be erased. If a scribe accidentally writes one of these in a Torah scroll and makes a mistake, he can't just reach for an eraser; the entire section must be cut out and placed in a "Genizah" (a respectful storage for holy papers until they can be buried). 📜 This law also means you have to be mindful of where you write these Names. We avoid writing them on anything that might be thrown away or treated disrespectfully—which is exactly why you'll often see people write "G-d" with a dash or use abbreviations like 'יי' in Hebrew books.
