There have even been documented cases of people writing Hashem's name on blackboards in schools, and the rabbis ruled that the entire blackboard needed to be buried! 📧 Even the "holy nicknames" we use for Hashem, like "The Merciful One," must be treated with dignity, though they don't have the same strict prohibition against erasing as the core seven. 🕊️ It’s a powerful exercise in mindfulness, reminding us that even the letters on a page can carry a spark of the Infinite!

Learn about the Melacha of Soter—the deliberate act of dismantling or demolishing a structure—and discover how this creative boundary guides how we handle household items, temporary setups, and container openings on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where the Levites carefully disassembled the massive wooden panels and foundation sockets of the Tabernacle whenever the Cloud lifted, preparing the components to be transported and perfectly rebuilt at the next wilderness camp. The core definition of Soter is the intentional dismantling, breaking down, or undoing of any stable structure, vessel, or functional unit. Under Torah law, this applies when the deconstruction is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to rebuild, improve, or clear space for a new structure. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically restrict destructive dismantling as well, introducing highly practical guidelines for everyday household objects. For instance, halacha directly guides how we handle temporary shelters; dismantling a large outdoor shade canopy or taking down a multi-panel tent on Shabbat can violate Soter, as undoing a covered shelter reverses the act of building. Similarly, unscrewing a loose door handle completely or forcefully taking apart modular plastic furniture units that were tightly locked together falls under this restriction. Learn more!

The very first act of each day:

What it is:
Modeh Ani (מוֹדֶה אֲנִי — literally "I am grateful") is a two-line prayer said the moment you open your eyes. It is the most basic expression of Jewish gratitude — thanking G-d for returning your soul after sleep.

The tradition:
Jewish thought teaches that sleep is a nightly experience of near-death — our soul partially leaves the body each night and is returned in the morning. Modeh Ani acknowledges that G-d chose to give you another day.

The Hebrew text and translation:
"Modeh ani lifanecha, Melech chai v'kayam, shehechezarta bi nishmati b'chemla — raba emunatecha."
"I thank You, living and eternal King, for returning my soul to me with compassion — how great is Your faithfulness."

How to do it:
Say it while still in bed, before getting up, immediately upon waking from your primary nighttime sleep — before doing anything else. Modeh Ani is one of the only parts of davening you can say without washing your hands first — then proceed to Netilat Yadayim (negel vasser; see the next item).

Nighttime sleep vs daytime naps:
You may repeat these words anytime as personal gratitude or mindfulness, but that is not the official morning prayer and does not carry the same halachic status.

Traditional practice reserves Modeh Ani for waking from major nighttime sleep. After a daytime nap, it is not required and is typically not recited — simply wash your hands (negel vasser) per the rules in the next item.

Learn about the Melacha of Matir—the deliberate act of untying a knot—and the fascinating halakhic symmetry that determines when opening a bundle becomes a forbidden labor on Shabbat! As the direct counterpart to the Melacha of Koshair, untying is rooted in the construction of the Mishkan, where craftsmen had to untie the permanent knots securing the heavy tapestries and ropes whenever the camp packed up to travel through the desert. On Shabbat, Matir prohibits the dismantling of any knot that falls under the category of a permanent or long-term bond. The rule creates a mirror image of the tying laws: any knot that is a Torah-level violation to tie is a Torah-level violation to untie, and any knot that is Rabbinically forbidden to tie is likewise forbidden to untie. This means that if you encounter a tight, professional knot that was meant to stay tied indefinitely—such as a heavily bound cargo cord or a specialized knot on a leather strap—you cannot untie it on Shabbat, even if it was originally tied years ago during the week. This law has direct practical effects on everyday activities, from opening a stuck knot on a package of food to handling tangled shoelaces. If something tightens accidentally into a knot (on Shabbat or even before), we are generally lenient to untie it, because it was never intended to be a knot. Tying something not meant to last more than 24 hours like a simple single (not double) shoe lace is also permitted. Learn more!

Learn about Shemittah — the seventh year when the land of Israel rests — and what this actually means for Jews living in Israel today! 🌾 Every seven years, the Torah commands that the land of Israel must rest: no plowing, planting, pruning, or commercial harvesting. Generally you're only allowed to do basic things to keep plants alive but not to improve them. Produce that grows on its own during Shemittah has 'kedushat shevi'it' (seventh-year sanctity) and can't be sold normally. The challenge: how does a country of millions survive without farming for a year? Two main halachic solutions are used today in Israel: (1) 'Heter Mechira' — a legal sale of Israeli farmland to a non-Jew for the year, so technically a non-Jew 'owns' the land during Shemittah and can farm it. The Chief Rabbinate permits this; many orthodox authorities do not. (2) 'Otzar Beit Din' — a rabbinical court takes formal ownership of the crops and distributes them, charging only for labor costs, not for the produce itself. Products with Otzar Beit Din certification need to be treated with Shemittah sanctity in your home (eaten and not wasted or destroyed). If the produce rots on its own that's fine, but you can't add it to the garbage and thus intentionally make it inedible. Many people use special "Shemittah bins" that let the food decompose naturally if they're not going to eat it. Authorities debate whether it's a positive mitzvah to enjoy holy Shemittah fruit. Many say it is! Learn more.

The Torah commands: "Be fruitful and multiply" — Pru u'Revu (פרו ורבו, Bereishit 1:28). Jewish men are obligated to marry and father children (Yevamot 61b; Shulchan Arukh, Even HaEzer 1:1).

Fulfilling the obligation:
A man fulfills pru u'revu once he has at least one son and one daughter who survive, each capable in principle of having children of their own (Yevamot 62a).

Marriage within the covenant:
The wife must be Jewish (Shulchan Arukh EH 2:1). Children born to a non-Jewish woman are not considered his under halacha; Jewish identity follows the mother (EH 4:19; Yevamot 17a). A man is expected to build a Jewish home — not to remain unmarried by choice (Rambam, Ishut 15:16).

A child born to a Jewish woman outside marriage may count toward the mitzvah in principle, but relations outside marriage are forbidden (EH 178; Devarim 23:18). Marriage is the proper path.

Beyond the minimum — la'arev ba'olam (לערב בעולם):
After the Torah obligation is met, the Sages teach a further mitzvah to continue having children — to populate the world with Jewish souls (Yevamot 62b; EH 1:5–8; Rema EH 1:8). How far to pursue this depends on health, circumstances, and personal psak.

When children are not possible:
One who cannot have children despite reasonable effort may be exempt — consult a qualified rav (EH 1:5–6). Poskim discuss infertility treatment, adoption, and other paths; rulings differ. The obligation to seek marriage remains even when fulfillment proves difficult.

This year, $chagName meets Shabbat on the calendar — you need eruv tavshilin. This doesn't happen every year.

Why (Peninei Halakha 12:8):
• On Yom Tov, cooking is generally allowed only for that calendar day of Yom Tov, not for the next day.
• When Yom Tov is immediately followed by Shabbat, the Sages required eruv tavshilin so you may cook on Yom Tov for Shabbat, and so Shabbat is not forgotten amid festival preparations.

$whenLine

How (Peninei Halakha 12:8:2):
• Set aside a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (meat, fish, or unpeeled hard-boiled egg are common examples).
• Recite the blessing and declaration from your Machzor or siddur in any language you understand — use the printed wording; do not paraphrase from memory.
• One eruv per household is sufficient.
• Food must be appropriate to eat with bread; at least a kezayit of cooked food should remain until Shabbat prep is done.
• Storage warning: Put the designated eruv foods in a safe, clearly labeled spot. If the eruv foods are eaten or destroyed before you finish cooking for Shabbat on Friday afternoon, your permission to prepare food is canceled (ask your rav if this happens).

Limits:
• Permits cooking and food prep on Yom Tov for Shabbat only — not cooking on one day of Yom Tov for the next festival day.
• Food should be ready early enough Friday afternoon that it could theoretically be eaten before Shabbat.
• Does not permit non-food Shabbat prep (e.g. certain laundry) — ask your rav.

Rosh Chodesh (ראש חודש) — the New Month — is a semi-holiday with extra prayer and customs.

Festive meal (mitzvah):
• It is a mitzvah to increase your meal on Rosh Chodesh — at minimum add an extra dish or special food in honor of the day (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 419:1).
• Have the meal during the day. Poskim write this commemorates the feast the Sanhedrin held at Beit Ya'zek for witnesses who came to testify they saw the new moon (Mishnah Rosh Hashanah 2:5; Orchos Chaim and Kol Bo, cited on O.C. 419).
• Money spent on Rosh Chodesh meals — like Shabbat and Yom Tov — is not deducted from the income allotted to you on Rosh Hashanah; if you spend more for these mitzvos, Heaven adds to your allotment (Pesikta de-Rav Kahana, cited in Tur O.C. 419 and Magen Avraham 419:1).

Davening today (listed in your Morning, Afternoon, and Evening Prayer sections):
• Yaaleh V'yavo in Shacharit, Mincha, and Maariv Amidah — and in bentching when you eat bread
• Half Hallel at Shacharit (Full Hallel if Rosh Chodesh falls during Chanukah; no Hallel on Rosh Chodesh Tishrei / Rosh Hashanah)
• Musaf after Shacharit — remove tefillin before Musaf
• Tachanun is omitted all day

Other customs:
• Fasting and eulogies are generally not done on Rosh Chodesh
• Widespread custom: married women refrain from certain melacha (needlework, laundry, etc.) as an extra mark of honor — ask your rav for details

When Rosh Chodesh spans two days (30th of the previous month and 1st), observances apply to both days.

Learn about Tochen — grinding and reducing substances to small particles on Shabbat! ⚙️ In the Mishkan, this melacha was performed by crushing herbs and minerals into fine powders to create the vibrant dyes for the tapestries and components for the incense. Because the primary category of Tochen applies strictly to things that grow from the earth (Gedulei Karka), dicing vegetables like Israeli salad into exceptionally fine pieces on Shabbat can constitute a direct violation unless you deliberately chop them into significantly larger pieces than your standard weekday cut. This earth-only definition is also why you are forbidden from scraping or crushing hardened, dried mud off your shoes on Shabbat, as breaking down caked soil into dust directly mimics the agricultural act of grinding. Conversely, because there is no grinding restriction for items that do not grow from the ground, you are permitted to finely chop or grate foods like cooked meat or cheese for immediate use. For pre-ground earth products, the halacha provides the exemption that 'there is no grinding after grinding' (Ein Tochen Achar Tochen), allowing one to crush pre-baked items like matzah or crackers into crumbs. This is why people rely on pre-ground coffee, avoid using blenders, change their vegetable chopping sizes, and handle muddy boots carefully on Shabbat, transforming meal preparation and daily upkeep into a profound lesson in refraining from dismantling the structural integrity of creation. Learn more!

A series of short blessings said at the beginning of the morning prayer service, thanking G-d for things we tend to take completely for granted.

What they are:
Birchot HaShachar (בִּרְכוֹת הַשַּׁחַר — Blessings of the Dawn) is a sequence of ~15 blessings at the start of Shacharit. They originated as blessings said at each stage of waking up and preparing for the day — as you open your eyes, stand up, put on clothes, and step outside.

What you thank G-d for:
• Giving intelligence to the rooster to distinguish day from night
• Not making you a non-Jew, a slave, or a woman (men's version; women say "for making me according to His will")
• Opening the eyes of the blind
• Clothing the naked
• Straightening those who are bent
• Spreading the earth over the waters
• Giving strength to the weary
• And more...

What comes before Birchot HaShachar:
In the full morning service, Korbanot (passages about Temple offerings) typically come before or after these blessings. While these may be skipped if time is short, saying them is an important part of the complete service.

Timing:
• The earliest time to say Birchot HaShachar is from chatzos (halachic midnight / chatzos halayla).
• L'chatchila (ideally), say the first blessing — HaNoten LaSechvi Vinah (who gives the rooster understanding to distinguish day from night) — after alot hashachar (dawn). If you said it after chatzos, you have still fulfilled your obligation.
• The other blessings in the sequence may be said from chatzos onward if you wake up very early.

Ready to launch into Shabbat with holiness? 🍷 Learn about Kiddush, the beautiful mitzvah that sanctifies our holy day! This isn't just a ritual; it's a Torah-level commandment to 'remember the Shabbat day to sanctify it' (Exodus 20:8). The Sages beautifully established this with Kiddush over a cup of wine. Here's how to rock your Kiddush, even if you're out of wine: (1) Kiddush B'Makom Seudah: You *must* recite Kiddush exactly where you'll be eating your meal! Making Kiddush in the living room and then sprinting to the kitchen for challah can be a real halachic oopsie! (2) Minimum Drink: You need to drink at least a 'melo lugmav' (approximately a cheekful or 2 fl oz.) of the Kiddush beverage. (3) Women's Obligation: Ladies, you're equally obligated! Even though it's a time-bound positive commandment (which often exempts women), women were part of the Exodus miracle that Shabbat commemorates. (4) Grape Juice: Yes, grape juice is perfectly valid for Kiddush and fully satisfies the obligation for everyone! (5) Kiddush Alternatives: The rules for alternatives differ between Friday night and Shabbat day. On Friday night, if wine/grape juice isn't available, **challah (bread)** is the next best option (make sure to wash hands first and say 'HaMotzi' in place of 'Borei Pri HaGafen'). On Shabbat morning, however, challah *cannot* be used as a substitute for Kiddush; instead, an 'important beverage' (chamor medina) like beer, whiskey, or even coffee/tea in certain communities (check with your Rav!) can be used!

Learn about the little known melacha of Maisach—the intricate art of warping and setting up the vertical base threads on a loom—and the deep structural lessons behind why even the infrastructure of production must pause on Shabbat! In the ancient textile world, before a single cross-thread could be woven to create a garment, a weaver had to meticulously arrange the 'warp'—a parallel grid of long, high-tension vertical threads stretched tightly across the loom frame. In the construction of the Mishkan, this precise setup was essential for creating the massive, multi-layered tapestries that formed the sanctuary walls and the heavy partition curtains. The core definition of Maisach is this act of organizing, separating, and anchoring these foundational threads into a rigid, functional matrix so that they are ready to receive the horizontal 'weft' threads. On Shabbat, this means that any act of setting up a loom, threading a warping board, or even stretching out a series of parallel lines to create a structural framework for weaving or braiding is strictly prohibited. What makes Maisach so profound is that it doesn't involve creating a finished piece of cloth; it is entirely about preparation and setting up the hidden infrastructure that makes future creation possible. By forbidding this preparatory stage, the Torah teaches us that Shabbat rest requires us to cease not just the final, visible acts of work, but even the behind-the-scenes planning, organizing, and structuring of our creative ambitions. Learn more!

Learn how to bid farewell to Shabbat with Havdalah! 🕯️🍷🌿 Havdalah (meaning 'separation') is the beautiful ceremony performed after Shabbat ends (Saturday night, once three stars are visible in the sky), marking our transition back to the ordinary weekdays. It's a multi-sensory experience with four distinct blessings, each connected to a specific element: (1) Wine: We recite 'Borei pri hagafen' over a full cup of wine, just like Kiddush, symbolizing the holiness that enters and exits with Shabbat. (2) Fragrant Spices (Besamim): We smell sweet spices while reciting 'Borei minei vesamim.' Why? The Talmud teaches that we receive an 'extra soul' (neshama yeteira) on Shabbat, and when it (with Shabbat) departs, the fragrant spices comfort our soul. (3) Fire: We say 'Borei m'orei ha'eish' over a lit candle with multiple wicks (like a special braided Havdalah candle), gazing at the flame and the shadows it casts on our fingernails. This blessing commemorates Adam's discovery of fire after the first Shabbat. (4) Havdalah Blessing: Finally, we recite 'Hamavdil bein kodesh l'chol,' praising Hashem who distinguishes between the holy and the mundane, light and darkness, Israel and the nations, Shabbat and the six days of work. A crucial halacha: you cannot perform any Melachah (labor forbidden on Shabbat) after Shabbat ends until you hear Havdalah or say the phrase 'Baruch Hamavdil bein Kodesh l'chol' (Blessed is He Who separates between holy and mundane). So say that even before you pick up the Havdalah items! Learn more!

Learn about Meraked — sifting and straining on Shabbat! 🫖 While Meraked achieves a similar result to Borer and Zoreh by separating desired items from undesired ones, the Talmud treats it as a distinct creative labor because it was a unique step in building the Mishkan, where a sieve was used to sift flour for the Showbread or to strain crushed herbs used for tapestry dyes. Today, Meraked specifically involves using a dedicated utensil, like a sifter or filter, to separate food or liquids from unwanted mixtures. A common toladah of this melacha is Meshamer (straining liquids), which carries a direct Torah-level prohibition if the liquid is so full of sediment or coffee grounds that the average person would completely refuse to drink it without filtration. Conversely, if a drink is already perfectly satisfactory and drinkable as it is, such as pouring orange juice through a mesh filter to remove pulp, the halacha permits it because the mixture is viewed as a single, acceptable entity rather than a mixture of two separate things. To avoid issues at the table, common activities to bypass on Shabbat include making coffee with a French press, pouring crushed matzah through a sifter to make matzah meal, using a slotted spoon to separate watery salads, or straining salad dressing directly from vegetables. This is why meals are intentionally prepared beforehand, reminding us that on Shabbat we step back from processing our physical world with tools and instead accept our food exactly as it has been prepared. Learn more!

Why learn at night — what the sages promise:

Nighttime Torah study has a special place in Jewish tradition. The Talmud praises one who establishes words of Torah at night — the verse "I rise at midnight to thank You" applies to him (Tehillim 119:62; Shabbat 119a). After the day's noise, learning at night is quiet cleaving to Hashem: the Shechinah dwells wherever words of Torah are spoken (Avot 3:2–3).

Our rabbis teach that Torah study protects the learner — it is counted among the three guardians of a person's life (Sotah 21a). Words of Torah are described in Kabbalistic tradition as ascending and forming advocates on high. Even a short night seder before sleep can settle the mind, sanctify the end of the day, and surround your household with merit.

Torah study is equal in weight to all other mitzvot combined (Peah 1:1). You do not need hours — consistency matters more than length.

The obligation:
Every Jew should learn at least a little Torah during the day and also at night. There is no upper limit to how much you may learn — the more Torah you study, the greater the mitzvah.

This checklist item is for nighttime study (from nightfall until the end of the night's obligations). Daytime study is tracked separately.

What counts:
Any authentic Torah learning — Chumash, Tanakh, Mishnah, Talmud, halacha, Mussar, Chassidut, or a structured daily program (such as Chitas for those who follow that custom).

Even a few minutes of focused learning at night fulfills this mitzvah. Consistency matters more than length.

Learn about treifot — internal defects that make a properly slaughtered animal forbidden to eat, even if everything else was done right! 🫁 An animal can be a kosher species, killed by a certified shochet with a flawless blade — and STILL be forbidden if it has certain physical defects. The lungs are the main focus: a trained inspector (bodek) examines the lungs after every single slaughter. A perforated lung is the most common treifah found. Many communities follow the 'glatt kosher' standard (from the Yiddish word for 'smooth') — meaning the lungs must be completely smooth with zero adhesions at all. Technically, some lung adhesions can be peeled off and examined, and if they peel cleanly the animal is still kosher (this is what 'regular kosher' means in many contexts). But the higher 'glatt' standard accepts only perfectly smooth lungs with no adhesions whatsoever. Other defects that make an animal treif: a punctured windpipe or esophagus, a broken spine, a missing or punctured kidney, a perforated stomach or intestine. The Talmud lists 18 main categories of treifot. The Rambam rules the list is CLOSED — nothing new can be added. This is why every piece of kosher meat you buy has been individually inspected by a trained professional. It's not a rubber stamp — real animals are failed and rejected every single day in every kosher slaughterhouse. An animal could be perfectly healthy to eat by USDA standards but still be treif. Think about what it means that Hashem only wants Jews to eat this level quality of meat.

Learn about one of the most surprising 'superpowers' in a kosher kitchen: the Case of the Spicy Onion! In the world of kosher law, flavor usually needs high heat to travel from a pot or a knife into your food. If everything is cold, tastes don't transfer and a simple rinse-off solves any mix-up (as long as it doesn't sit for 24 hours). But there is a fascinating exception called Davar Charif — literally 'a sharp thing' — where the rules of physics seem to change. Think of foods like onions, lemons, or hot peppers as having a sort of chemical heat. These foods are so 'aggressive' that they don't need a stove to move flavor around. If you take a cold knife that is only used for meat and slice into a sharp, raw onion, that onion actually 'squeezes' the meat essence out of the steel blade. Even though no one turned on a flame, that onion is now considered a meat product. If you accidentally tossed it into a bowl of cream cheese, you'd have a real kosher conundrum on your hands! What makes Davar Charif even more 'powerful' is how it handles time. Usually, if a pot or knife hasn't been used for 24 hours, any flavor trapped inside its pores is considered 'stale' and loses its status. But a sharp food can actually 'reawaken' that sleeping flavor. A sharp onion can pull flavor out of a knife that hasn't touched a steak in years, making the onion 'meat' all over again. This is why many people keep a special 'Pareve' knife — one that is neither meat nor dairy — specifically for their davar charif fruits and veggies. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Mocheik—the deliberate act of erasing or wiping away meaningful marks— on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled scribes would immediately erase or scrape off any mistaken lettering on the wooden wall beams or parchment skins to perfectly prepare the surface to be written upon correctly. The core definition of Mocheik is the intentional wiping, scraping, dissolving, or obliterating of any meaningful symbol, letter, or graphic. Under Torah law, this applies when erasing is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to clean a surface so it can be written on again, like wiping a whiteboard or slate clean. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically forbid destructive erasing where no rewriting occurs. This boundary directly guides how we handle written or printed materials on the day of rest, meaning that scratching out text, erasing pencil marks, or deliberately destroying a permanent record is prohibited. For Ashkenazi communities following the Rema, this restriction leads to a preference to avoid tearing through printed letters or logos when opening food packages from the outset, though if done accidentally or after the fact (b'dieved), it's permitted. For Sephardic communities following Maran Rav Ovadia Yosef, tearing through packaging text to access food or medication is permitted from the outset, as the destructive act is done with zero intent to erase or rewrite text. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Mav'ir—the deliberate act of kindling or fueling a fire—and discover how this creative boundary completely pauses our control over heat and combustion on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor is so central that the Torah singles it out explicitly, tracing its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans kindled fires to melt metals for the Tabernacle's vessels and to bake offerings. The core definition of Mav'ir is the intentional creation, expansion, or fueling of any flame, spark, or intense heat source. Under Torah law, this applies to striking a match, adding wood to a bonfire, or stepping on the gas pedal of an internal combustion engine car. While a person is unlikely to be forging metal on Saturday, Mav'ir introduces highly practical rules for modern technology. Halacha rules that completing an electrical circuit to turn on an appliance or lightswitch, adjusting a dial to raise a gas burner, or plugging in an electronic device is restricted, as creating a live electrical current or heating an element mimics or violates the strict boundaries of kindling a fire. This boundary directly shapes how we navigate modern hotels, manage kitchen appliances, and use electricity. To navigate this smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals set automated timers to control home lights before the day begins, set slow-cookers to a steady, preset temperature before Friday evening, and rely on pre-existing warmth and light to honor the day of rest. Learn more!

Learn about the melacha of Toveh—the ancient art of spinning thread and twisting loose fibers into functional yarn—and the deep spiritual mechanics behind why we let the spinning wheels rest on Shabbat! This sophisticated category of labor is the bridge between raw materials and actual textiles, tracing directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where the artisan women famously exhibited extraordinary skill by spinning the goat hair while it was still attached to the living animals. The core definition of Toveh is taking loose, short, disorganized masses of wool, cotton, or flax and drawing them out while twisting them tightly to fuse them into a single, continuous, and durable strand. On Shabbat, this means any action that creates a thread, yarn, or cord from raw material is strictly prohibited, whether you are using an industrial spinning wheel, a handheld drop spindle, or just rolling fibers together between your palms. The prohibition also extends to twisting multiple existing threads together to create a thicker rope or heavy twine, which is a sub-category of the labor. What makes Toveh so profound is that it targets the precise moment where separate, useless bits of fluff are permanently transformed into a structured tool of creation; it represents the ultimate act of human manufacturing and manipulation of nature. By pausing this process on Shabbat we acknowledge that we do not need to constantly spin the raw elements of the world into tools of production, allowing us to step away from the loom of weekly labor. Learn more!

After using the bathroom, we wash our hands and say the Asher Yatzar blessing — every time you finish.

When to say it:
• Normally: say Asher Yatzar each time you use the bathroom, once you are done and have washed your hands.
• If you are ill — for example diarrhea or another condition where you may need the bathroom again right away — wait until you are reasonably sure you have finished for that round before saying it. Otherwise you might say the blessing and then need the bathroom again immediately, which is impractical and not the point of the bracha.

What it is:
Asher Yatzar (אֲשֶׁר יָצַר — "Who fashioned") is a blessing thanking G-d for the miraculous design of the human body. It is one of the most medically insightful prayers ever composed.

The blessing says:
G-d created the human body with wisdom — with openings and closings (tubes, valves, sphincters). If any one of them were to open when it should be closed, or close when it should be open, it would be impossible to survive.

Why this is profound:
Modern medicine has confirmed exactly this. A blocked ureter, a stuck heart valve, or a blocked bowel — any of these is a life-threatening emergency. Every time your body functions normally, it is a miracle. Asher Yatzar trains us to recognize it as one.

How to do it:
1. After using the bathroom, wash your hands (without a blessing, unless this is your morning washing)
2. Say Asher Yatzar — it takes about 20 seconds

Find the text in any Jewish prayer book (siddur) or app. In Mitz Mode, tap the menu (⋮) → **Blessings** for the text.

Learn about the ultimate 'Heavenly delivery service' that sustained a nation for forty years! ☁️ The Manna was a supernatural food that fell from the sky every morning, and the halacha surrounding it is absolutely wild. It was the original "clean eating" because it was perfectly absorbed by the body with zero waste! 🥯 Every person received exactly one "omer" (about 43 eggs' worth of volume), no matter how much they tried to hoard. If you tried to save some for the next day, it would miraculously spoil—teaching the nation the ultimate lesson in trusting Hashem for their daily bread! 🕊️ But on Fridays, the "Double Portion" fell, giving everyone enough for Shabbat without any work. This is actually where we get the custom of having two loaves of Challah at our Shabbat tables today! 🍞🍞 The midrash says it tasted like whatever you imagined—from honey cake to a savory steak—making it the most versatile menu in history. 🍯🥩 It only stopped falling once the Jewish people crossed the Jordan River and entered the Land of Israel. Did you know? To make sure we never forgot this miracle Hashem commanded Moshe to place a golden jar of Manna inside the Holy of Holies next to the Ark! 🏺 When King Yoshiyahu hid the Ark to protect it from invaders before the Temple was destroyed, he hid the jar of Manna right along with it where it remains hidden to this day. When Moshiach comes we hope we'll merit to see it again and say the bracha on manna: 'HaMotzi Lechem Min HaShamayim' (Who makes bread comes forth from the Heavens)! Talk about a breakfast of champions!

Jewish law applies in the marketplace and office just as in the synagogue. Honest weights, fair deals, and trustworthy conduct are Torah obligations — not optional "extra credit."

What this includes:
• Geneivat da'at (גְּנֵבַת דַּעַת — deception): misleading a customer or employer about price, quality, terms, or your qualifications.
• Ona'ah (אוֹנָאָה): overcharging or underpaying when the other party does not know the fair value. A variation of one-sixth or more above or below fair market price triggers the Torah laws of financial fraud, requiring the overcharged amount to be fully refunded.
• Keeping your word: honoring agreements, deadlines, and commitments; not making promises you cannot keep.
• Paying workers on time: wages owed must be paid when due (Leviticus 19:13).
• Not stealing in any form — including time theft, expense fraud, or taking what is not yours.
• Taxes and debts: fulfilling legal obligations honestly; not cheating the government or private parties.

Key idea:
"In righteousness you shall judge your fellow" (Leviticus 19:15) — business dealings are judged by the same moral standard as ritual mitzvot. A Jew's integrity (yosher — יֹשֶׁר) is a sanctification of G-d's name in the world.

How to practice:
• Be transparent in sales, billing, and negotiations.
• When in doubt, ask a rabbi or trusted advisor about a specific contract or industry practice.
• Treat employees, clients, and employers with the dignity the Torah demands.

Check this off as a daily intention to act with honesty in all financial and professional interactions.

Speech is one of the most powerful forces in Jewish life — it can build people up or destroy them in an instant. Shmirat halashon (שְׁמִירַת הַלָּשׁוֹן — guarding the tongue) is the ongoing mitzvah to watch what we say.

What to avoid:
• Lashon hara (לָשׁוֹן הָרָע — "evil tongue"): saying true or untrue derogatory words about another person — their character, their business, their children, and the like — even without intent to harm. If the information would lower the listener's opinion of that person, it is generally forbidden. Speaking lashon hara about the Land of Israel (Eretz Yisrael) is also forbidden.
• Rechilut (רְכִילוּת): tale-bearing — passing words from one person to another in a way that creates conflict.
• Ona'at devarim: hurtful or humiliating speech, insults, mockery, and needless argument.
• Falsehood and misleading others in speech.

Why it matters:
The Talmud compares lashon hara to murder — reputations and relationships can be killed without raising a hand. The Chafetz Chaim (Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan, 19th century) devoted his life to teaching these laws because they protect the dignity of every Jew.

How to practice:
• Before sharing news about someone, ask: Is it necessary? Is it kind? Would I want this said about me?
• When you hear gossip, do not repeat it; you may change the subject or gently defend the person spoken about.
• Use speech for encouragement, truth, prayer, and Torah.

This checklist item is a daily reminder to be mindful — not a scorecard of perfection. Ask your rabbi when a specific situation is permitted (e.g., warning someone of harm).

Learn about shechitah — kosher slaughter — and the five requirements that must all be met or the meat is forbidden! 🔪 The five invalidating factors of shechitah, remembered by the Hebrew acronym ShaCHaT NaDaH: (1) Shehiyah — pausing mid-cut. The cut must be one continuous smooth motion. Stop in the middle, even briefly, and it's potentially treif. (2) Chaladah — 'burrowing.' The knife cannot go underneath the trachea into the throat; it must cut cleanly across the surface, and the knife must be completely visible. It cannot be inserted under a flap of skin or between the trachea and esophagus (3) Drasha — pressing too hard. The knife must GLIDE, not be forced. If the shochet pushes rather than draws the blade, it's invalid. (4) Hagramah — cutting in the wrong location. There's a very precise section of the throat where the cut is valid — too high or too low and it's invalid. (5) Ikur — tearing. The windpipe or esophagus cannot be ripped out before the cut is complete. And the knife itself? It must be razor-sharp with a perfectly smooth edge — no nicks. The knife is so sharp it can put a high-quality chef’s knife or surgical scalpel to shame! Those often have a tiny, saw-like jaggedness. That microscopic 'saw' is actually what helps a kitchen knife bite into a tomato skin. For a chalaf, that jaggedness is strictly forbidden. The blade must be polished to a mirror-like, frictionless finish. If there is even a miniscule burr, nick, or unevenness on the edge, the knife will tear the animal's tissue rather than slice it cleanly, instantly rendering the meat non-kosher. Kosher meat is a high 'steaks' biz! 😂

Learn about the melacha of Me'ameir (gathering) and how a simple pile of fruit can become a violation of Shabbat rest! Most people think of 'work' as building something complex, but Me'ameir is actually one of the earliest steps in the agricultural process, tracing back to when workers in the Mishkan collected fallen stalks of grain or gathered herbs to make dyes for the tapestries. The core idea is taking scattered items that grew from the ground and bringing them together into a single unit or pile. What makes this melacha so interesting is how specific the rules are: for it to be a forbidden act of gathering, the items must have grown from the ground, you must be gathering them in the place where they originally grew, and it must be the natural way of processing them for use. For example, if you are in your backyard and a bunch of apples have fallen off a tree, you cannot go around and pick them up into a basket because by grouping them together, you are performing a constructive act in the cycle of food production. However, if those same apples have already been gathered once and then they spill all over your kitchen floor, you are allowed to pick them up because the work of Me'ameir is considered finished — you aren't creating a new pile, you're just cleaning up a mess. This rule even applies to non-food items, like gathering fallen wood in a forest to make a pile for a fire. It teaches us that on Shabbat, we leave the world exactly as it lies, refusing to even organize nature's scattered pieces so that we can allow the earth to remain in its own natural state while we enjoy the sanctity of the day. Learn more!

Melave Malka (מְלַוֶּה מַלְכָּה) means "escorting the queen" — a meal Saturday night to escort the Shabbat Queen as she departs, the way one would honor a beloved guest at the door (Talmud Shabbat 119b). It is also called Seudat David HaMelech: King David held a Motzei Shabbat feast each week after learning he would die on Shabbat (Shabbat 30a).

When:
After Havdalah, from tzeit (nightfall) until dawn Sunday. Men and women; all nusachim.

What to do:
• Spread a clean tablecloth and set the table — the sages say to set the table even if you will eat only a small amount
• Sit and eat at the table. The ideal is a meal with bread: wash hands (netilat yadayim), say hamotzi, and eat. If you are too full for bread, you can still fulfill the mitzvah with cake or another meaningful portion of food — netilat yadayim applies only when you eat bread
• When you can, prepare something for this meal rather than only finishing Shabbat leftovers
• Many light candles on or near the table

Four candles (optional — Baal Shem Tov, Siddur Tzelota D'Yisrael; Sefer Amrei Esh):
Some light four candles at Melave Malka. Chassidic tradition teaches that King David is spiritually present at this meal. A common practice (Rabbi Aaron Roth, Shulchan HaTahor): dedicate one candle each to King David, Eliyahu HaNavi, Rabbi Meir Baal HaNess, and the Baal Shem Tov — or choose any four tzaddikim. Light them at the table, name each dedication, and ask then for your needs for the week ahead in the merit of the tzaddikim.

If you are truly too full from Shabbat, you are not required to force yourself — but even a little food at a set table honors the mitzvah.

Learn about the Melacha of Mechabeh—the deliberate act of extinguishing a fire—and discover how this creative boundary completely pauses our control over flames and heating elements on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled metalworkers and artisans would extinguish burning wood embers to produce pure charcoal for smelting metals or to control the heat of ovens used for baking offerings. The core definition of Mechabeh is the intentional putting out, dimming, or dampening of any glowing flame, spark, or fire. Under Torah law, this applies when extinguishing is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to create charcoal or save a wick to burn again later. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically forbid any destructive extinguishing as well, introducing highly practical rules for the modern home. On Shabbat, this means that blowing out a candle, pouring water on a burning wick, or turning off a gas stove flame is strictly forbidden. Even turning down a live burner or lowering a flame to simmer food is restricted, as dimming a fire is a direct form of partial extinguishing. While a person is unlikely to be managing open forge fires on Saturday, Mechabeh shapes how we handle lighting and cooking appliances. To navigate this smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals light all candles before the day of rest begins and leave them to burn out naturally, set automated timers to control electric lights, and leave stove burners at a steady, preset temperature under a protective cover (blech) until the week begins again. Learn more!

Mincha is the afternoon prayer — brief, powerful, and often said in the middle of the working day.

What Mincha is:
Mincha (מִנְחָה — originally the name of the afternoon grain offering in the Temple) is the afternoon prayer service. It was instituted by our patriarch Yitzchak (Isaac), who went to the field to pray in the afternoon (Genesis 24:63, per the Talmud).

What the Mincha service contains:
• Ashrei (Psalm 145) — see morning prayer notes
• Amidah / Shemoneh Esrei — the same 19-blessing standing prayer (see above)
• Tachanun — on most weekdays (see below)
• Aleinu — a closing prayer affirming G-d's sovereignty over the world

Tachanun (weekdays):
Tachanun (תַּחֲנוּן — supplication) is a penitential prayer after the Amidah on most ordinary weekdays. It is omitted on Rosh Chodesh, Yom Tov, Chanukah, Purim, and other days of joy.

At Shacharit:
• Mondays and Thursdays: longer Tachanun (often opening with "Vehu Rachum" / וְהוּא רַחוּם).
• Other weekdays: shorter form (exact text varies by nusach).

At Mincha:
Usually the shorter version — often beginning with "Vayomer David el Gad" ("And David said to Gad..."). If you already said Tachanun at Shacharit, Mincha may still include Tachanun depending on your custom — follow your siddur or congregation.

Timing:
Mincha begins from about 30 minutes after midday and must finish before sunset. The app shows the current window. If you miss Mincha, it can be made up at Maariv only — say the Maariv Amidah first, then immediately say a second Amidah as the tashlumin. This window closes when Maariv ends; you cannot make it up the following morning. See the guide entry on Tashlumin for full rules.

Birkat Hachamah (בִּרְכַּת הַחַמָּה — Blessing of the Sun) is recited once every 28 years, when the sun completes the machzor gadol (מחזור גדול) — the great 28-year solar cycle described in the Talmud (Berakhot 59b).

The cycle:
Jewish tradition calculates the solar year as 365¼ days (Samuel; Eruvin 56a). Each year the vernal equinox shifts forward six hours; after 28 years it returns to the same day of the week and time — Tuesday at sundown, when the sun was created (Bereishit 1:14–19). Because the blessing requires seeing the sun, it is recited the following morning — Wednesday at sunrise.

This occurrence: $dateLabel.

The blessing:
Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam oseh ma'aseh bereishit.
(בָּרוּךְ אַתָּה ה' אֱלֹקֵינוּ מֶלֶךְ הָעוֹלָם עוֹשֵׂה מַעֲשֵׂה בְרֵאשִׁית)
"Blessed are You, L-rd our G-d, King of the universe, Who makes the works of creation."

How to fulfill it:
• Go outdoors where the sun is visible — not through a window, per most poskim.
• Recite at sunrise on the morning of the occurrence, when you can see the sun. Many communities gather for a public recitation with additional Tehillim and passages — follow your kehilla.
• Ideal time: from sunrise through the third halachic hour of the day (sof zman tefillah). According to some opinions, you may still recite it until chatzos (halachic midday).
• If the sky is completely overcast and the sun cannot be seen, many hold the blessing is not recited without visible sunlight; some communities still gather when clouds hide the sun.

Notes:
• This mitzvah comes only once every 28 years.
• Check your local synagogue or Chabad center — large communal gatherings are common.

Learn about the Melacha of Mechateich—the deliberate act of cutting a material to a specific size or shape—and discover how this precise manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we open packages, prepare food, and handle household items on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans carefully measured and cut heavy woven tapestries, leather skins, and wooden beams to exact lengths and dimensions to ensure every single piece fit perfectly into the Tabernacle structure. The core definition of Mechateich is the intentional cutting, tearing, or trimming of any functional object to a specific, measured size, geometric shape, or precise dimension for a productive purpose. On Shabbat, this means that trimming a piece of plastic wrap to a specific length, cutting a loose thread to even out a fabric border, or sawing a wooden board to size for a DIY shelf is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be cutting building materials on Saturday, Mechateich introduces highly practical guidelines when it comes to routine household chores. A classic example is tearing toilet paper along its perforated lines; because you are deliberately tearing the paper at a measured, pre-cut boundary to get a specific size sheet, it is a direct violation of Mechateich (even NOT cutting on the lines is a problem of the melacha of Koreah). Similarly, snapping apart a multi-pack of yogurt cartons that are attached to each other at a perforated plastic seam falls under this restriction, as it cleanly divides the material into precisely sized individual units. Learn more!

Yom HaShoah V'HaGevurah (27 Nisan) is the national day of remembrance for the six million Jews murdered in the Holocaust. It was established by the Israeli Knesset in 1953.

This is not a Yom Tov — melacha is fully permitted. It is a national civil observance, not a halachically mandated fast or prayer day.

Date adjustment: If 27 Nisan falls on Friday, the day is observed on Thursday (26 Nisan). If it falls on Sunday, it is observed on Monday (28 Nisan), to avoid disruption of Shabbat.

Customs by community:

In Israel: Two-minute siren sounds at 10:00 AM; most Israelis stop and stand in silence. Memorial ceremonies are held at Yad Vashem and throughout the country.

Prayers: Standard weekday davening — Yom HaShoah does not add or remove any siddur insertions. It is a Knesset civil memorial, not a rabbinically instituted liturgical day; Religious Zionist / Dati Leumi communities do not omit Tachanun specifically because of Yom HaShoah. (27 Nisan falls in Nisan — Tachanun is omitted throughout the entire month of Nisan per Shulchan Arukh O.C. 429:2, the universal standard for Ashkenazim and Sephardim alike; that is a separate rule of the joyous month, not this observance.) Some communities hold memorial learning or ceremonies.

Charedi communities: Many do not observe this date as a religious memorial, preferring 10 Tevet (designated by the Chief Rabbinate in 1949 as Yom Kaddish HaKlali for those whose date of death is unknown) or Tisha B'Av as the appropriate day of mourning for all Jewish tragedies. This is a matter of minhag and communal leadership.

Chabad: No official communal observance is instituted, though the memory of the kedoshim (holy martyrs) is honored.

Mechirat chametz (מְכִירַת חָמֵץ) — selling your chametz to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach — lets you keep chametz products locked away without owning them during Pesach (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 448).

Why:
• On Pesach, owning chametz is forbidden (bal yera'eh / bal yimatzei). Selling transfers ownership so sealed chametz in your home does not belong to you during the festival.

How to do it:
• Sign or authorize a sale with your rabbi or community (online forms are common). Deadline warning: While the sale takes effect on Erev Pesach morning, you must authorize your rabbi to sell your chametz several days in advance — most rabbis stop accepting sale forms by the night before Erev Pesach. The rabbi needs time to organize contracts and complete the kinyan (legal transfer) before the halachic deadline.
• Mark cabinets or rooms included in the sale; keep sold chametz separate from what you will burn or discard.
• Do not eat or use sold chametz after the sale takes effect — it belongs to the buyer until buy-back after Pesach.
• Dishes used with chametz: Many people include the chametz residue and absorbed flavor within their year-round dishes and pots in the sale, locking them away securely. The physical dishes themselves are not sold, avoiding the halachic requirement to re-immerse them in a Mikveh (tevilat kelim) after Pesach (Shulchan Arukh Y.D. 120). Rabbis structure the contract accordingly — follow your rabbi's sale form.
• Store the contract or confirmation; many communities sell through a central rabbi (e.g. local kashrut council).

After Pesach: chametz is repurchased per the terms of the sale — follow your rabbi's instructions on when you may use it again.
$urgency

Simchas Yom Tov (שִׂמְחַת יוֹם טוֹב) — the mitzvah to rejoice on the festival:
The Torah commands "V'samachta b'chagecha" — "And you shall rejoice in your festival" (Devarim 16:14). Joy on Yom Tov is a mitzvah for everyone. Halacha recognizes that happiness is personal — the head of household helps each family member rejoice in the way that naturally brings them joy (Pesachim 109a; Shulchan Arukh O.C. 529:2).

Prepare before chag begins:
• Wife — clothing or jewelry l'fi mamono (within your financial ability): the Shulchan Arukh obligates a husband to buy his wife new clothes or jewelry for Yom Tov. You are not expected to go into debt — an inexpensive but pleasant garment, modest jewelry, or nice shoes fulfills the mitzvah. The goal is her actual joy; if she prefers something else that genuinely makes her happy (a book, houseware, experience), many modern poskim agree that fulfills the spirit of the law.
• Children — treats that bring joy: the Talmud mentions roasted grains and nuts; today candies, chocolates, and age-appropriate toys. The purpose is to associate Yom Tov with excitement and a break from everyday routine — not merely to give sugar.
• Men — meat and wine: in Temple times this meant holiday sacrifices; today festive Yom Tov meals with wine and meat (beef is classic; poultry is often acceptable) fulfill personal simchas Yom Tov.

Rambam's crucial condition (Hilchos Yom Tov 6:18):
When you feed your family, buy your wife clothes, and give your children treats, you must also provide for the poor, the widow, and the orphan. If a household feasts only behind closed doors without helping the needy, the Rambam writes this is not "the joy of a mitzvah" but merely "the joy of one's stomach."

Before you open a book — remember what Torah study is:

The blessings you are about to say exist because Torah is how we cleave to Hashem. The sages teach that learning Torah outweighs every other mitzvah combined (Peah 1:1), that the Shechinah joins those who speak words of Torah (Avot 3:2–3), and that Torah study protects your life in this world and the next (Sotah 21a). Birkat HaTorah is the doorway into that reality — asking Hashem to make Torah sweet on your lips, not heavy.

Every day, before learning Torah for the first time, we say Birkat HaTorah — the blessings over Torah study. For men, this is widely held as a Torah-level commandment; women's obligation and its level are debated among poskim — all agree women who learn Torah should say these blessings.

What these blessings are:
Birkat HaTorah (בִּרְכַּת הַתּוֹרָה — blessings of the Torah) consists of three short blessings:
1. A blessing thanking G-d for commanding us to engage in Torah study
2. A blessing asking G-d to make the words of Torah sweet in our mouths and the mouths of our children
3. A blessing praising G-d for giving us the Torah

After saying these blessings, we immediately read short Torah passages (which also fulfills a minimum daily Torah study obligation).

Why they matter:
The Talmud teaches that many troubles in Jewish history came from failing to say the blessings before Torah study. These blessings express that Torah is not just intellectual — it is a divine gift and our most sacred inheritance.

Timing:
Said once in the morning; they cover all Torah learning for the entire day. They can be said from after halachic midnight (chatzos halayla — the midpoint of the night) onward — so even if you wake up very early, say them.

Learn a fascinating halachic debate about whether raisins need to be checked for bugs! 🍇 The Torah states in Leviticus 11:41, 'And every swarming thing that swarms upon the earth is a detestable thing; it shall not be eaten.' Chazal and the Rishonim clarify that this specific phrasing—'upon the earth'—suggests that an insect generated inside a fruit might be treated differently if it never actually swarms on the ground. ⚖️ The core of the 'machloket' is this: Detached Produce: If an insect develops in fruit after it has been harvested (detached), many authorities rule it is permitted as long as it never leaves the fruit. However, the moment it exits—even if it just moves to the surface and never touches the ground—it becomes forbidden, and crawling back inside does not change that! 🚫 Attached Produce: If an insect develops while the fruit is still attached to the tree or vine, the law is much stricter. Many major authorities, including the Rambam, rule that these insects are forbidden even if they never leave the fruit, because they are already considered to have 'swarmed' while the plant was part of the earth. Because raisins are dried grapes, some rely on a leniency associated with the 'Taz,' suggesting that any potential insects likely developed after the grapes were detached and never emerged. On the flip side, many are strict because insects could have developed while the grapes were still on the vine, or they might have crawled out during the drying process. 🧺 This is why you will see such a range of practice, from those who snack freely, to those who only use raisins with the highest level of 'checked' certification! Fascinating! Learn more about the important mitzvah of checking fruits and veggies!

The week before Shavuot is for practical preparation — Shavuot celebrates Matan Torah (receiving the Torah at Sinai).

Food & home:
• Dairy is a cherished Shavuot minhag (cheesecake, blintzes, lasagna, ice cream) — plan menus and shop early. A festive meat meal with wine remains standard practice to fulfill the primary mitzvah of Simchat Yom Tov (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 529:2); many families have a dairy kiddush or snack, then a full meat Yom Tov meal.
• Some decorate with flowers and greenery (minhag of a garden around Sinai).
• Stock wine, grape juice, and Yom Tov staples for festive meals${if (!profile.isInIsrael) " — in the Diaspora, prepare for two days of Yom Tov" else ""}.

Torah & tefillah:
• Confirm shul times for Maariv, Shacharit, and Musaf; many communities have all-night learning (Tikkun Leil Shavuot) — find a program or plan a home study session.
• Choose texts to learn: Ruth (read on Shavuot), Megillat Rut customs, Pirkei Avot, or a topic your family enjoys.
• Review Akdamut / Yizkor customs if your community observes them on the second day (Diaspora).

Joy & family (simchat Yom Tov):
• Plan festive meat meals with wine — the core Simchat Yom Tov obligation — alongside any dairy minhag.
• Gifts for wife and children (clothes, treats) l'fi mamono — associate the day with joy.

Practical:
• On Erev Shavuot: daytime preparations can continue up until nightfall (tzeit), as the holiday begins later than usual. Reheating and cooking for the night meal may also be done on the holiday itself after tzeit, strictly from a pre-existing flame. Set up a blech or hot plate if needed. You cannot light candles or make Kiddush until tzeit.
• Turn off devices before Yom Tov — this app is for prep, not use on chag.

Learn about the Melacha of Shochait—the act of slaughtering or taking the life of a living creature—and how this serious prohibition connects directly to everyday actions like flossing your teeth on Shabbat! This creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where animals were slaughtered for the sanctuary sacrifices and to process their skins for the heavy coverings that shielded the Tabernacle structure. The core definition of Shochait is the termination of life, and on Shabbat, it applies strictly across the entire animal kingdom, meaning that hunting, slaughtering, or killing any mammal, bird, fish, or insect is completely forbidden (unless it's danger to human life). Because the concept of Shochait centers on the extraction of life-sustaining fluid from a body, the Talmud expands this definition to include Chavurah—the act of causing a wound that draws blood from a living being, which is viewed as a partial form of taking life.  If a person has sensitive gums and knows with absolute certainty that flossing will cause their teeth to bleed, sliding that floss between their teeth becomes a direct violation of the prohibition against causing a wound on Shabbat. Because of this, authorities rule that if flossing is guaranteed to draw blood, it must be avoided on the day of rest. It shows that the spirit of Shochait is not just about avoiding major hunts or slaughterhouses; it is about cultivating a deep reverence for life and vitality in all its forms. By leaving the animal kingdom unbothered and ensuring our own bodies remain unwounded, we honor a day dedicated entirely to peace, vitality, and preservation, relying completely on the food and preparations we completed during the week.

Learn about the Melacha of Potze'a—the deliberate act of separating, unraveling, and undoing woven threads—and the fascinating reasons why picking at a loose strand can be a violation of Shabbat rest! While most people understand that creating a piece of cloth is an act of labor, the Torah teaches that strategically undoing that same fabric is equally creative. This category of work traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where weavers would occasionally make mistakes in the complex, dual-faced tapestry patterns of the sanctuary curtains, or find damaged sections that required them to carefully slice, unravel, and separate the interlaced warp and weft threads so the material could be salvaged and re-woven properly. The core definition of Potze'a is this specific, constructive undoing of a woven or braided structure, where the goal is to return the fabric to its individual, usable strands or to clear space for a new pattern. On Shabbat, this means that systematically pulling apart a knitted blanket, unraveling the edge of a frayed piece of cloth to harvest the yarn, or separating the woven elements of a basket is strictly forbidden. What makes Potze'a so surprising in daily life is how it applies to everyday clothing mishaps, like that sudden urge to tug on a loose thread hanging from the hem of a sweater or jacket. If pulling that thread unravels a row of stitches or separates the interlaced fibers of the garment, it directly mirrors the ancient dismantling process used by the Mishkan's craftsmen. The rule teaches us that on Shabbat, we refrain from both building up and breaking down the material world, inviting us to leave our garments exactly as they are and to appreciate a day where nothing needs to be corrected, unraveled, or reformed. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Koteiv—the foundational prohibition against writing—and discover how this creative boundary shapes how we act on Shabbat! This central category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled builders wrote distinct letters or symbols on the wooden wall beams to mark their matching pairs, ensuring the massive structure could be disassembled and reassembled with perfect accuracy. The core definition of Koteiv is the formation of any meaningful symbol, character, or letter that conveys information upon a surface. To violate the Torah prohibition, a person must write at least two distinct letters or symbols using a permanent medium on a lasting surface. However, the Sages extended this boundary to protect the day of rest, rabbinically forbidding temporary writing and shaping any letters or images that don't pre-exist. Similarly, placing a torn page of a book back together so the letters line up can violate Koteiv. Even books with words or stamped designs printed directly onto the outer edges of the pages present a unique question, as opening and closing the book repeatedly splits and reunites the text, leading many authorities to prefer using standard books without edge-printing on the holy day. This boundary also guides how we play games like Scrabble; while simply placing loose, flat letter tiles side-by-side on a table is generally permitted, playing with travel-style games where the letters physically click, snap, or lock into a rigid frame or grid is questioned by authorities, as firmly securing letters into a backing is similar to writing. As for keeping score, rabbis who are concerned players may be accustomed to writing down their scores, suggest using a (not holy) book to track scores by flipping the pages to match their score!

Bedikat chametz (בְּדִיקַת חָמֵץ) — the formal search for chametz — is a rabbinic mitzvah on the night **before** Erev Pesach day (after tzeit when the Hebrew date becomes 14 Nisan).

$whenLine

How to search:
• Use a candle (or flashlight per many poskim), a wooden spoon, and a feather (or paper bag) to gather crumbs.
• Search every room where chametz may have been brought during the year — especially kitchen, dining areas, living room, car, office, children's bags, and coat pockets.
• Check under furniture, cushions, car seats, and appliances where crumbs collect.
• Place ten pieces of bread (wrapped) in rooms before searching if your custom includes finding known pieces (optional minhag). If you hide them, write down every location and verify all 10 are recovered — a missed piece means known chametz remains in your home.

After the search:
• Recite the blessing Al bi'ur chametz and the Kol chamira nullification (bitul) — many siddurim print the text.
• Text difference: Use the night version of Kol Chamira from your siddur. It nullifies only chametz you have not seen and do not know about — because you may still legally own chametz for breakfast tomorrow morning.
• When Erev Pesach is Shabbat, this first bitul is Thursday night after bedikat; the final Kol Chamira is on Shabbat morning before the end of the 5th halachic hour — not at Friday's burning.
• Gather found chametz in a bag to destroy the next morning at biur chametz.
• Eating restrictions: You may not eat a meal or start work after nightfall until you complete the search. Once the search is finished, you may eat normally. Tomorrow morning is biur chametz — avoid a heavy meal from midday (chatzos) onward to preserve your appetite for the Seder.

If you are traveling or staying elsewhere, your host or rabbi can guide which rooms you are responsible to search.

Learn about the Melacha of Tofair—the precise art of sewing—and how a single stitch can become a profound lesson in Shabbat rest! This creative labor traces back to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans sewed together the vast sheets of woven goat hair and sheep wool to form the massive protective coverings for the desert sanctuary, and stitched the intricate fabrics of the priestly garments. The core definition of Tofair is the joining together of two separate pieces of flexible material—like cloth, leather, or paper—into a unified, single entity using a third connecting element like thread, string, or glue. On Shabbat, this means that passing a needle and thread through a garment to mend a tear, or even pulling a broken seam back together, is strictly forbidden. What makes Tofair so fascinating in modern life is that the prohibition is not limited to traditional needle and thread; it applies to any action that creates a lasting, continuous bond between separate objects. This includes using liquid glue to paste pages together, applying heavy duct tape to bind two surfaces, or even using a stapler to lock papers into a permanent file. When a sudden fashion emergency strikes on Saturday—like a popped button, a ripped hem, or a split seam on your Shabbat clothes—the solution requires a careful understanding of these boundaries. Because the law targets permanent, creative connections, you cannot use tape or glue, but you can use a temporary safety pin to hold the fabric together, provided it is pinned in a casual, temporary way that does not mimic actual tailoring. It transforms a minor weekend mishap into a moment of mindful awareness, showing that on Shabbat, we let our wardrobe and our world remain exactly as they are, trusting that a temporary patch and a sense of humor are all we need to honor the day. Learn more!

Learn about the world's first mandatory RSVP event - the Korban Pesach! 🐑 This wasn’t just a mitzvah, it was a logistical miracle. Imagine hundreds of thousands of people descending on Jerusalem all with one goal: barbecuing enough meat to feed the entire nation! 🌃 But you couldn't just stroll in and buy a couple lamb chops. You had to be registered on a specific group's animal before the sun even went down. Every single person had to be part of a 'chaburah' or registered group. If you weren’t on the list you weren’t eating! 📝 On the afternoon of the 14th of Nissan pretty much every able-bodied Jew in the country showed up at the Temple at once. To handle this holy rush hour, the priests split everyone into three massive shifts. As soon as the first group packed the courtyard the heavy doors swung shut and the service began!  This is where it gets cinematic because the Kohanim (priests) stood in perfectly orchestrated lines reaching all the way to the altar. One row held only gold bowls and the next only silver. As lambs or goats were offered, the blood was passed hand to hand down the line like a relay race! Once your animal was slaughtered and its blood sprinkled on the altar, you got your animal back. Then each group went to where they were staying, and needed to roast their meat whole, entirely over an open fire and hold a Pesach seder! And forget the carving knife because you were strictly forbidden from breaking a single bone! It had to stay whole and regal. 🦴 The pressure was on to finish by the midnight deadline while the whole city erupted in the singing of Hallel. Jerusalem was so packed the rooftops were covered in people and the smell of roasted lamb filled the air! It was a massive roaring celebration of freedom! May we merit to experience it ourselves speedily in our days as soon as the Temple is rebuilt! Learn more! 🎊🍷

Learn about the melacha of Koshair—the precise art of tying permanent knots—and the fascinating halakhic boundaries that separate a casual double-knot from a forbidden act of creation! This category of labor traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where camel drivers and craftsmen tied thick, permanent knots to secure the massive fabric tapestries to the wooden beams, and fishermen tied and repaired the nets used to catch the snails that provided the rare blue chilazon dye for the techelet color. The core definition of Koshair is the creation of a tight, durable, and professional-grade knot that is intended to remain intact long-term. On Shabbat, the law divides knots into three main categories based on two major factors: the professional skill required to tie it, and the duration the knot is meant to last. A knot that is both professionally crafted and meant to be permanent—like a specialized sailor's knot or an artisan's knot on a leather strap—is strictly forbidden by the Torah itself. On the flip side, a completely temporary, amateur knot that is meant to be undone within twenty-four hours—like tying a plastic garbage bag or securing a bag of bread with a simple single-knot—is totally permitted because it lacks the permanence required to be considered a true act of construction. The gray area lies in semi-permanent knots meant to last for a week or a few days, which Rabbinic law prohibits to ensure people do not accidentally cross into tying permanent ones. This creates unique scenarios for everyday life, especially when it comes to tying shoes. While a standard bow on a sneaker is perfectly fine because you untie it at the end of the day, tightening it with a tight, permanent double-knot that stays tied for weeks would be a serious issue. It teaches us that on Shabbat, we refrain from binding the physical world together in a lasting way. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Oreg—the iconic art of weaving threads together—and the fascinating boundaries that separate the appreciation of fabric from its creation on Shabbat! Weaving is the definitive climax of the textile production process, tracing directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where master craftsmen interlaced the vertical warp threads with horizontal weft threads to build the heavy, majestic tapestries that enclosed the sacred sanctuary. The core definition of Oreg is the act of crisscrossing or interlacing two or more distinct sets of threads or yarns at right angles to form a coherent, self-supporting sheet of fabric. On Shabbat, this means that any form of traditional loom-weaving, darning a hole in a sock by interlacing new threads over a gap, or even pulling a single loose thread back into an existing woven garment to restore its pattern can be a direct violation of this labor. What makes Oreg so sensitive is that it does not require a massive machine or a complex project to be triggered; the Talmud states that weaving even two parallel horizontal threads into a vertical grid is enough to violate the prohibition. This carries huge implications for modern fiber arts and everyday mishaps, as certain types of dense embroidery, cross-stitching, or even tighter forms of basketry and wire-weaving share the exact same structural mechanics as loom-work and must be paused on Shabbat. The deep spiritual essence of Oreg teaches us that while the creation of cloth is one of humanity's most fundamental achievements in mastering nature for clothing and shelter, Shabbat is a day where we completely step away from fabrication. We wear our beautiful clothes and admire the woven textures around us, but we entirely relinquish the power to interlace even a single thread, honoring a day where the fabric of the universe is already considered complete. Learn more!

Kashrut is the system of Jewish dietary laws that governs what we eat, how food is prepared, and how meat and dairy are kept separate.

What kosher means:
Kosher (כָּשֵׁר) literally means "fit" or "proper." Food that meets all the Torah's dietary requirements is called kosher. Food that does not is called treif (טְרֵיף — literally "torn," referring to torn-apart animals).

The main rules:

Permitted and forbidden animals:
• Land animals must have split hooves AND chew their cud: beef, lamb, and goat are kosher; pork is not
• Fish must have fins AND scales: salmon, tuna, and carp are kosher; shrimp, lobster, and catfish are not
• Chicken, turkey, duck, and other domestic fowl are kosher

Shechita (kosher slaughter):
• All meat must be slaughtered by a shochet — a specially trained and G-d-fearing slaughterer — using a swift, painless cut. This is called shechita (שְׁחִיטָה)
• After slaughter, the meat is salted to remove blood (melicha), since consuming blood is forbidden

Separating meat and dairy (basar b'chalav):
• Meat and dairy may never be eaten together, cooked together, or served on the same dishes
• After eating meat, you wait a period of time before dairy. Six hours is standard in many communities, but customs vary (some wait one or three hours) — follow your rabbi and community
• Separate sets of dishes, pots, and utensils are kept for meat and dairy

Hechsher:
• A hechsher (הֶכְשֵׁר) is a certification mark on packaged foods indicating rabbinical supervision. Look for a recognized symbol on labels.

Why these laws:
The Torah gives these laws as divine commands. Beyond the reason, observing them elevates every meal into an act of service to G-d and builds discipline and consciousness in our daily lives.

Getting started:
Consult your local Orthodox rabbi before setting up a kosher kitchen. Many people begin by removing non-kosher meat and seafood, then work toward full observance.

Learn about the Melacha of Memachek—the delicate art of smoothing and scraping surfaces—and discover how this finishing-touch boundary directly alters the way we use soap, toothpaste, and lotions on Shabbat! This meticulous category of creative labor traces its origins directly to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled craftsmen scraped raw animal hides with precision tools to remove hairs, eliminate rough bumps, and sand down imperfections until the leather was perfectly uniform and supple for the Tabernacle coverings. The core definition of Memachek is the mechanical smoothing, sanding, or scraping of any stiff, solid material—such as wood, stone, metal, or leather—to level out its surface and make it completely even. On Shabbat, this means that sanding down a piece of furniture, scraping a rough leather strap to smooth it out, or polishing a scratch out of a wooden surface to improve its texture is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be sanding wood or processing raw hides on a Saturday, Memachek has a massive, highly practical counterpart in daily life called Memarei'ach, which is the act of smoothing down malleable, pasty substances. This sub-category directly shapes personal hygiene and medical care on the day of rest, specifically when it comes to using thick creams, ointments, bar soaps, or even toothpaste. Halacha rules that spreading a thick layer of ointment over a wound, rubbing a bar of solid soap until it lathers smoothly, or squeezing a thick ribbon of toothpaste across a brush closely mirrors the ancient act of leveling a surface to make it flush. To avoid this boundary on Shabbat, observant individuals alter their routine by using liquid soap instead of bar soap, applying liquid oils or thin lotions that instantly absorb into the skin rather than thick creams, and gently dabbing a necessary medical ointment onto an injury without spreading or smoothing it flat. Learn more!

Learn about the Melacha of Oseh Batei Nirin—the intricate art of creating heddles, harnesses, and loops on a loom—and how the precision engineering of ancient textile production shapes our understanding of Shabbat rest! To understand this sophisticated category of labor, one must visualize how a loom operates: the vertical warp threads cannot simply sit flat; they must be threaded through individual loops or eyelets called heddles, which are attached to movable frames or harnesses. By raising or lowering these frames, the weaver splits the warp threads into two separate layers, creating a temporary tunnel or 'shed' through which the horizontal cross-threads can easily pass. In the construction of the Mishkan, this precise setup was absolutely vital for weaving the complex, dual-faced tapestries that required intricate geometric and animal patterns to be visible on both sides of the curtain. The core definition of Oseh Batei Nirin is the formation or setting up of these guiding loops, whether by tying individual strings to form the eyelets, threading the warp strands through existing heddles, or attaching the heddle-frames to the loom's mechanics. On Shabbat, this means that any act of assembling the guiding apparatus of a weaving machine, creating loops that function as a track for other strands, or repairing the intricate rigging of a loom is strictly prohibited. What makes this Melacha so fascinating is that, like its predecessor Maisach, it is completely non-functional on its own; it is an act of pure structural calibration designed solely to facilitate the upcoming weaving process. By pausing this mechanical setup on Saturday, the Torah demonstrates that true rest encompasses the cessation of all preparatory engineering and technical calibration, encouraging us to step away from the fine-tuning of our creative tools and simply exist in harmony with a world that is already perfectly aligned by the ultimate Craftsman.

Learn about the fascinating laws of Nedarim—the binding power of vows—and discover how a person's spoken words can physically alter legal reality, creating a mind-bending halakhic puzzle that requires a piece of genuine legal time-travel to solve! 📜 Under Torah law, human speech behaves like a reality-warping tool; if a person declares an item forbidden to themselves, they legally transform that ordinary object into a prohibited item, making its use a direct biblical violation. Because people often make rash promises in moments of anger, the Torah provided a legal escape hatch through Hatarat Nedarim (the nullification of vows) by a Torah sage who can dissolve the vow entirely. However, this setup leads to a hilarious legal deadlock codified by the Rambam in Mishneh Torah, Hilchot Nedarim 4:13. 🤯 Consider a man who gets into a heated dispute with a local community and storms off after swearing: 'I forbid myself from deriving any benefit from the people of this city!' When his temper cools and he desperately regrets his words, he finds himself caught in a massive spiritual trap: the only local sage capable of releasing his vow is a resident of that forbidden city. If he asks the local rabbi for help, the act of receiving rabbinic guidance means he is actively deriving a benefit from a resident—violating the exact vow he is trying to escape! 🏛️ The Rambam elegantly resolves this logical paradox by dividing the timeline into two distinct realities. As the Rambam states in Chapter 4, Halachah 13, initially, it is forbidden for the person to ask such a sage to release his vow, for in this way, he is benefiting from the people of the city.
